Next lesson: Circle Equations and Intersections.

screens.title: Standard form reveals the centre and radius

diagram.caption: Every point on the circle is distance r from the centre (a, b).

screens.body:
The circle with centre [[(a,b)]] and radius r has equation

[[(x-a)^{2}+(y-b)^{2}=r^{2}]].

The signs inside the brackets are opposite to the centre coordinates, and the right-hand side is the radius squared.
screens.keyPoint: Read [[(x+3)^{2}]] as [[(x-(-3))^{2}]]: its centre coordinate is -3.

screens.question: What are the centre and radius of [[(x+3)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}=25]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The centre is [[(-3,2)]] and [[r=\sqrt{25}=5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Reverse the bracket signs for the centre, then take the positive square root of the right-hand side.

screens.title: Convert expanded form into standard form
screens.body:
A circle may be written [[x^{2}+y^{2}+cx+dy+e=0]]. Group the x-terms and y-terms, then complete each square.

Use [[x^{2}+cx=\left(x+\frac c2\right)^{2}-\left(\frac c2\right)^{2}]], and similarly for y.
screens.keyPoint: Whatever constants you add to complete the squares must be balanced on both sides.

screens.title: Reveal the centre and radius
screens.question: Find the centre and radius of [[x^{2}+y^{2}-6x+4y-12=0]].

diagram.caption: Completing both squares converts the expanded equation into geometric information.

steps.title: Collect the constants
steps.body: [[(x-3)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=25]]. The centre is [[(3,-2)]] and the radius is 5.

screens.finalAnswer: The centre is [[(3,-2)]] and the radius is 5, so the correct option is B.

screens.question: Find the centre and radius of [[x^{2}+y^{2}+8x-2y+8=0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The equation becomes [[(x+4)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=9]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete both squares: add 16 for the x-terms and 1 for the y-terms.

screens.title: You can extract geometry from either form of a circle equation.
screens.body:
You have read standard form and completed squares in expanded form.

Next, we test points, intersect circles with lines, and construct a circle from a diameter.

screens.title: Subtract two circle equations

diagram.caption: The common chord passes through both intersection points of the circles.

screens.body:
Suppose two circles intersect at points A and B. Every point on both circles satisfies both equations.

Subtracting one circle equation from the other eliminates the [[x^{2}]] and [[y^{2}]] terms. The remaining linear equation is the line through A and B, called the common chord.
screens.keyPoint: For two equal circles, the common chord is also the perpendicular bisector of the line joining their centres.

screens.question:
The circles

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-4x+6y-12=0]]

and

[[x^{2}+y^{2}+8x-2y-8=0]]

intersect at two points. What is the equation of the line through those intersection points?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Subtracting the second equation from the first gives [[-12x+8y-4=0]], which simplifies to [[3x-2y=-1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Subtract the complete circle equations. The [[x^{2}]] and [[y^{2}]] terms cancel, leaving the equation of the common chord.

screens.title: Substitution produces a quadratic

diagram.caption: Two, one, or zero real quadratic roots correspond to a secant, tangent, or non-intersecting line.

screens.body:
Substitute the line equation into the circle equation. The resulting quadratic gives the intersection x-values.

A positive discriminant gives two intersections, zero gives one tangent point, and a negative discriminant gives no real intersection.
screens.keyPoint: After solving for x, substitute into the line equation to recover each y-coordinate.

screens.title: Find both line-circle intersections
screens.question: Find the intersections of [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]] and [[y=x+1]].

diagram.caption: The line cuts the circle at two points, so the substituted quadratic has two roots.

screens.finalAnswer: The intersections are [[(3,4)]] and [[(-4,-3)]], so the correct option is A.

screens.question: Where does the line [[y=4]] meet the circle [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]]?
options: It does not meet the circle
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Substitution gives [[x^{2}+16=25]], so [[x=\pm3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Set [[y=4]] in the circle equation, solve for both x-values, and retain the fixed y-coordinate.

screens.title: Express the radius in terms of the parameter
screens.body:
When a circle equation contains a parameter, complete both squares as usual. The resulting right-hand side is [[r^{2}]].

If the question asks for the smallest possible radius, rewrite [[r^{2}]] in completed-square form and find where it is minimised.
screens.keyPoint: Minimising [[r^{2}]] also minimises r because the radius is non-negative.

screens.question:
For each real value of p, the equation

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-2px+4y+6p-9=0]]

describes a circle. For which value of p does the circle have its minimum possible radius?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Completing both squares gives [[(x-p)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=(p-3)^{2}+4]]. Therefore [[r^{2}]] is smallest when [[p=3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete the x-square and y-square, then rewrite the resulting expression for [[r^{2}]] as a square plus a constant.

screens.title: Circle Equations and Intersections: key points
keyPoints: [[(x-a)^{2}+(y-b)^{2}=r^{2}]] has centre [[(a,b)]] and radius r.
Complete the x-square and y-square separately in expanded form.
Subtract two circle equations to obtain the equation of their common chord.
Line-circle intersections reduce to a quadratic after substitution.
For a parameterised circle, complete the squares to express [[r^{2}]] in terms of the parameter.
reviewItems: Reverse bracket signs when reading centre coordinates.
Balance constants carefully while completing squares.
Use subtraction to eliminate the quadratic terms from two circle equations.
Use the quadratic discriminant to count line-circle intersections.
Complete the square in the parameter when finding a minimum or maximum radius.
Next lesson: Circle Theorems on Axes.

screens.title: A tangent is perpendicular to the radius at contact

diagram.caption: Join the point of contact to the centre before finding the tangent gradient.

screens.body:
To find a tangent equation, calculate the gradient from the centre to the contact point, then take its negative reciprocal.

The tangent passes through the contact point. If the radius is vertical, the tangent is horizontal; if the radius is horizontal, the tangent is vertical.
screens.keyPoint: First confirm that the stated contact point lies on the circle.

screens.title: Find a tangent without differentiation
screens.question: Find the tangent to [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]] at [[(3,4)]].

steps.title: Identify the centre
steps.body: The centre is [[(0,0)]]. The point [[(3,4)]] lies on the circle because [[3^{2}+4^{2}=25]].

screens.finalAnswer: The tangent is [[3x+4y=25]], so the correct option is A.

screens.question: A circle has centre [[(2,-1)]], and [[P=(5,3)]] lies on it. Which line is tangent at P?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The radius gradient is [[\frac{3-(-1)}{5-2}=\frac43]], so the tangent gradient is [[-\frac34]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the centre-to-P gradient, take its negative reciprocal, and use P in point-gradient form.

screens.title: The perpendicular from the centre bisects a chord

diagram.caption: The centre lies on the perpendicular bisector of every chord.

screens.body:
For chord endpoints A and B, its midpoint M is found by averaging coordinates. The line from the centre to M is perpendicular to AB.

Therefore an unknown centre can be located by intersecting perpendicular bisectors of chords or combining one such bisector with other information.
screens.keyPoint: A chord's perpendicular bisector passes through the circle centre even when the chord is not a diameter.

screens.title: Locate a centre from a chord
screens.question: A circle contains chord endpoints [[A=(2,6)]] and [[B=(8,2)]]. Its centre lies on [[y=x]]. Find the centre.

diagram.caption: The centre is where the chord's perpendicular bisector meets y = x.

steps.title: Intersect with the known centre line
steps.body: Set [[y=x]]. Solving [[x-4=\frac32(x-5)]] gives [[x=7]], so the centre is [[(7,7)]].

screens.finalAnswer: The centre is [[(7,7)]], so the correct option is D.

screens.question: A circle has radius 5 and a chord of length 8. What is the perpendicular distance from the centre to the chord?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The perpendicular bisects the chord, giving a right triangle with hypotenuse 5 and one leg 4. The other leg is [[\sqrt{25-16}=3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Halve the chord first, then use Pythagoras with the radius as the hypotenuse.

screens.title: You can combine gradients, midpoints, and circle structure.
screens.body:
You have constructed tangents and located centres from chords.

Next, we connect coordinate information with the remaining angle theorems.

screens.title: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference
screens.body:
For the same arc AB, [[\angle AOB=2\angle ACB]], where O is the centre and C is on the circumference.

Angles standing on the same chord in the same segment are equal. Coordinate information may first be needed to identify the centre, endpoints, or diameter.
screens.keyPoint: Check that the angles refer to the same arc or chord before applying a circle theorem.

screens.title: Translate between related angles

steps.title: Identify the common arc
steps.body: Suppose chord AB subtends [[34^{\circ}]] at a point C on the circumference.

steps.title: Use the centre theorem
steps.body: At the centre O, the same chord subtends twice that angle.

steps.title: Use the same segment
steps.body: Any other point on the same segment standing on chord AB also sees an angle of [[34^{\circ}]].

screens.question: One angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is 117°. What is the opposite angle?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°, so [[180^{\circ}-117^{\circ}=63^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the supplementary opposite-angle property of a cyclic quadrilateral.

screens.title: Diameters create right angles; tangents transfer angles
screens.body:
An angle subtended by a diameter at the circumference is 90°.

The angle between a tangent and a chord at the point of contact equals the angle made by that chord in the alternate segment. These facts can complete a triangle after coordinate work identifies a diameter or tangent.
screens.keyPoint: The radius-tangent right angle and the alternate-segment theorem are different facts; use the one matching the requested angle.

screens.title: Find a tangent from an external point
screens.question:
The circle [[(x-1)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=25]] has centre C. The point [[P=(8,-1)]] lies outside the circle.

Two tangents can be drawn from P. Which equation represents the tangent with negative gradient?

diagram.caption: At a point of contact T, the radius CT is perpendicular to the tangent PT.

steps.title: Find the distance from C to P
Use the right triangle
steps.body:
If T is a point of contact, then [[CT\perp PT]]. Since [[CT=5]], Pythagoras gives

[[PT^{2}=CP^{2}-CT^{2}=50-25=25]].

Therefore [[PT=5]].

steps.title: Form a second circle
steps.body:
The possible contact points T are distance 5 from both C and P. They therefore lie on both

[[(x-1)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=25]]

and

[[(x-8)^{2}+(y+1)^{2}=25]].

steps.title: Find the possible contact points
steps.body:
Subtracting the two circle equations gives [[7x+y=30]]. Substitution into either circle gives

[[x^{2}-9x+20=0]],

so [[x=4]] or [[x=5]]. The contact points are [[(4,2)]] and [[(5,-5)]].

steps.title: Choose the negative-gradient tangent
steps.body:
The line from [[P=(8,-1)]] to [[(4,2)]] has gradient

[[\frac{2-(-1)}{4-8}=-\frac34]].

Hence [[y+1=-\frac34(x-8)]], which rearranges to [[3x+4y=20]].

screens.finalAnswer: The required tangent is [[3x+4y=20]], so the correct option is A.

screens.question:
The points A, B, C and D lie on a circle in that order. A tangent is drawn at A.

The angle between the tangent and chord AB is [[(2x+12)^{\circ}]], [[\angle BAC=(x+8)^{\circ}]], and [[\angle ADC=(4x+10)^{\circ}]].

What is the value of x?

diagram.caption: Use the alternate-segment theorem, the angle sum of a triangle, and the cyclic-quadrilateral theorem.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The alternate-segment theorem gives [[\angle ACB=(2x+12)^{\circ}]].
So in triangle ABC:
[[\angle ABC=180^{\circ}-(x+8)^{\circ}-(2x+12)^{\circ}]]
[[=160-3x]].
Since ABCD is cyclic:
[[\angle ABC+\angle ADC=180^{\circ}]].
Hence:
[[(160-3x)+(4x+10)=180]].
So [[x=10]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First transfer the tangent-chord angle to [[\angle ACB]] using the alternate-segment theorem. Then find [[\angle ABC]] from triangle ABC and use the fact that opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to [[180^{\circ}]].

screens.title: Circle Theorems on Axes: key points
keyPoints: A tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the contact point.
The centre lies on the perpendicular bisector of every chord.
The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference on the same arc.
An angle in a semicircle is 90°, and opposite cyclic angles sum to 180°.
The tangent-chord angle equals the angle in the alternate segment.
reviewItems: Confirm contact points satisfy the circle equation.
Use midpoint and negative-reciprocal gradient for chord bisectors.
Name the common arc before applying an angle theorem.
Combine coordinate facts and geometric facts in separate clear stages.
Next lesson: Coordinate Geometry Exam-style Questions.

screens.question:
The lines

[[(p-1)x+2y=5]]

and

[[3x+(p+1)y=7]]

are perpendicular. What is the value of p?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Their gradients are [[-\frac{p-1}{2}]] and [[-\frac{3}{p+1}]]. Perpendicular gradients have product -1, so
[[\frac{3(p-1)}{2(p+1)}=-1]].
Hence [[3p-3=-2p-2]], giving [[p=\frac15]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write each line's gradient in terms of p, then set the product of the two gradients equal to -1.

screens.question: The lines [[2x-y=1]] and [[x+2y=8]] intersect at P. A line through P is perpendicular to [[3x-4y=7]]. Which equation represents this line?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The first two lines meet at [[P=(2,3)]]. The line [[3x-4y=7]] has gradient [[\frac34]], so the required gradient is [[-\frac43]]. Thus
[[y-3=-\frac43(x-2)]],
which rearranges to [[4x+3y-17=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First find the intersection point P. Then take the negative reciprocal of the gradient of [[3x-4y=7]].

screens.question: What is the equation of the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining [[(-2,5)]] and [[(6,-1)]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The midpoint is [[(2,2)]]. The segment gradient is [[-\frac34]], so the perpendicular gradient is [[\frac43]]. Therefore
[[y-2=\frac43(x-2)]],
which becomes [[4x-3y-2=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the midpoint and the segment gradient. The perpendicular bisector passes through the midpoint with the negative-reciprocal gradient.

screens.question:
For which value of p is the line

[[(p-2)x+(p+1)y=7]]

parallel to the y-axis?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. A line parallel to the y-axis is vertical, so its y-coefficient must be zero. Thus [[p+1=0]], giving [[p=-1]]. The x-coefficient is then -3, so the equation is genuinely vertical.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A vertical line has no y-term. Set the coefficient of y equal to zero and check that the x-coefficient is non-zero.

screens.question:
A regular octagon is inscribed in the circle

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-6x+8y+7=0]].

What is the area of the octagon?

diagram.caption: First extract the circumradius from the circle equation.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Completing the square gives
[[(x-3)^{2}+(y+4)^{2}=18]],
so [[r^{2}=18]]. Dividing the octagon into eight central triangles gives
[[8\left(\frac12r^{2}\sin45^{\circ}\right)=2\sqrt2r^{2}=36\sqrt2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete the square to find [[r^{2}]], then divide the regular octagon into eight isosceles triangles with central angle [[45^{\circ}]].

screens.question:
For each real value of p, the equation

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-2px+6y+8p-12=0]]

describes a circle. When does the minimum possible radius occur, and what is that radius?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Completing the squares gives
[[(x-p)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}=(p-4)^{2}+5]].
Hence [[r^{2}=(p-4)^{2}+5]], which is minimised when [[p=4]]. The minimum radius is [[\sqrt5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete both coordinate squares, then complete the square in p. Minimise [[r^{2}]], not the unsimplified equation.

screens.question:
Find the complete set of real values of p for which

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-2px-6y-p^{2}+6p+5=0]]

represents a genuine circle.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Completing the squares gives
[[(x-p)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}=2p^{2}-6p+4]]
[[=2(p-1)(p-2)]].
A genuine circle requires [[r^{2}>0]], so [[p<1]] or [[p>2]]. Equality would give only a single point.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete both squares and impose the strict condition [[r^{2}>0]]. A zero radius is not a genuine circle.

screens.question:
The circles

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-2x+4y-20=0]]

and

[[x^{2}+y^{2}+6x-2y-12=0]]

intersect at two points. What is the equation of the line through those points?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Every intersection point satisfies both circle equations. Subtracting them eliminates [[x^{2}]] and [[y^{2}]]:
[[-8x+6y-8=0]].
Dividing by -2 gives [[4x-3y+4=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Subtract one complete circle equation from the other. The quadratic terms cancel, leaving the common chord.

screens.question: Point P lies on [[(x+1)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}=9]]. Point Q lies on [[(x-5)^{2}+(y+6)^{2}=16]]. What is the maximum possible value of PQ?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The centres are [[(-1,2)]] and [[(5,-6)]], whose distance is
[[\sqrt{6^{2}+(-8)^{2}}=10]].
The maximum occurs when P and Q lie on the line of centres on the outer sides, so [[PQ=3+10+4=17]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the distance between the centres, then add both radii for the greatest possible separation.

screens.question:
For which values of k does the line [[y=2x+k]] intersect the circle

[[(x-1)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=20]]

at two distinct points?

diagram.caption: A line has two intersections exactly when its distance from the centre is less than the radius.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Write the line as [[2x-y+k=0]]. The centre is [[(1,-2)]] and the radius is [[2\sqrt5]]. Two intersections require the centre-to-line distance to be less than the radius:
[[\frac{|k+4|}{\sqrt5}<2\sqrt5]].
Thus [[|k+4|<10]], giving [[-14
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the perpendicular distance from the circle's centre to the line. A secant requires this distance to be strictly less than the radius.

screens.question: The circle [[(x-2)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=25]] has two tangents through [[P=(9,2)]]. Which equation is the tangent with negative gradient?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The line [[3x+4y=35]] passes through P and has gradient [[-\frac34]]. Its distance from the centre [[(2,1)]] is
[[\frac{|3(2)+4(1)-35|}{5}=5]],
equal to the radius, so it is tangent.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Test the candidate lines: the required line must pass through P, have negative gradient, and lie exactly one radius from the centre.

screens.question: A and B are endpoints of a diameter of a circle, where [[A=(-2,1)]] and [[B=(6,5)]]. What is the equation of the tangent at A?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The centre is the midpoint [[(2,3)]]. The radius from the centre to A has gradient [[\frac12]], so the tangent gradient is -2. Hence
[[y-1=-2(x+2)]],
or [[2x+y+3=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the midpoint of the diameter first. The tangent at A is perpendicular to the radius joining the centre to A.

screens.question: A circle contains the chord with endpoints [[A=(1,5)]] and [[B=(7,1)]]. Its centre lies on [[y=x+1]]. What is the centre?

diagram.caption: The centre lies on the perpendicular bisector of every chord.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The chord midpoint is [[(4,3)]] and its gradient is [[-\frac23]], so its perpendicular bisector has gradient [[\frac32]]:
[[y-3=\frac32(x-4)]].
Intersecting this with [[y=x+1]] gives [[x=8]] and [[y=9]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The centre lies on the chord's perpendicular bisector. Find that line, then intersect it with [[y=x+1]].

screens.question:
The line [[3x+4y=16]] cuts the circle

[[x^{2}+y^{2}-6x+4y-12=0]]

in a chord. What is the length of the chord?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The circle is [[(x-3)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=25]], so its radius is 5. The centre-to-line distance is
[[\frac{|3(3)+4(-2)-16|}{5}=3]].
Half the chord has length [[\sqrt{5^{2}-3^{2}}=4]], so the chord length is 8.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the radius and the perpendicular distance from the centre to the chord line. The perpendicular bisects the chord.

screens.question: A circle is tangent to the x-axis at [[(2,0)]] and passes through [[(0,2)]]. Which is its equation in expanded form?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The centre lies vertically above or below the contact point, so write it as [[(2,a)]] with radius [[|a|]]. Since [[(0,2)]] lies on the circle,
[[4+(2-a)^{2}=a^{2}]],
giving [[a=2]]. Thus [[(x-2)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}=4]], which expands to [[x^{2}+y^{2}-4x-4y+4=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the fact that the radius at the contact point is vertical. Let the centre be [[(2,a)]], then use the second point.

screens.question: The tangent to [[(x-1)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=25]] at [[(4,2)]] meets the positive coordinate axes. What is the area of the triangle enclosed by the tangent and the axes?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The radius from [[(1,-2)]] to [[(4,2)]] has direction [[(3,4)]], so the tangent is
[[3x+4y=20]].
Its intercepts are [[\frac{20}{3}]] and 5. Therefore the area is
[[\frac12\times\frac{20}{3}\times5=\frac{50}{3}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the tangent equation using the radius as a normal vector. Then use its x- and y-intercepts as the triangle's base and height.

screens.question: The chord AB subtends angles [[(3x+7)^{\circ}]] and [[(5x-17)^{\circ}]] at two points C and D in the same segment of a circle. O is the centre. What is [[\angle AOB]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Angles in the same segment are equal, so
[[3x+7=5x-17]],
giving [[x=12]]. Each circumference angle is therefore [[43^{\circ}]]. The angle at the centre on the same arc is twice this, so [[\angle AOB=86^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First equate the two same-segment angles. Then double the resulting circumference angle to obtain the central angle.

screens.question: AB is a diameter of a circle and C is another point on the circumference. The angle between the tangent at A and chord AC is [[38^{\circ}]]. What is the angle between the tangent at B and chord BC?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. By the alternate-segment theorem, [[\angle ABC=38^{\circ}]]. Since AB is a diameter, [[\angle ACB=90^{\circ}]], so [[\angle BAC=52^{\circ}]]. Applying the alternate-segment theorem at B, the angle between the tangent and BC is also [[52^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Transfer the first tangent-chord angle into triangle ABC, use the right angle made by the diameter, then apply the tangent-chord theorem again at B.

screens.question:
A, B, C and D lie on a circle in that order. The opposite angles satisfy

[[\angle BAD=(2x+5)^{\circ}]]

and

[[\angle BCD=(5x+7)^{\circ}]].

O is the centre. What is the angle at O standing on the same arc BD as [[\angle BAD]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to [[180^{\circ}]], so
[[(2x+5)+(5x+7)=180]],
giving [[x=24]]. Hence [[\angle BAD=53^{\circ}]]. The angle at the centre on the same arc is twice this, so it is [[106^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the cyclic-quadrilateral condition to find x. Then double the angle at the circumference to obtain the angle at the centre.

screens.question: The points A, B, C and D lie on a circle in that order, and a tangent is drawn at A. The angle between the tangent and chord AB is [[(3x+5)^{\circ}]], [[\angle BAC=(x+10)^{\circ}]], and [[\angle ADC=(5x+2)^{\circ}]]. What is x?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The alternate-segment theorem gives [[\angle ACB=(3x+5)^{\circ}]]. Therefore
[[\angle ABC=180^{\circ}-(x+10)^{\circ}-(3x+5)^{\circ}]]
[[=165^{\circ}-4x]].
Since ABCD is cyclic,
[[(165-4x)+(5x+2)=180]],
so [[x=13]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the alternate-segment theorem to find [[\angle ACB]], then the triangle angle sum to find [[\angle ABC]], and finally the cyclic opposite-angle condition.

screens.title: Coordinate Geometry: exam review
keyPoints: Use gradients, point-gradient form, and midpoint or distance formulae systematically.
Parallel lines share gradients; perpendicular gradients are negative reciprocals.
Convert expanded circles by completing both squares.
Substitute line equations into circles and interpret the quadratic roots geometrically.
Combine coordinate calculations with tangent, chord, and angle theorems.
reviewItems: Check signs when reading circle centres.
Treat vertical and horizontal lines explicitly.
Retain both roots when a line crosses a circle twice.
Verify contact points before constructing tangents.
Repeat this set until algebraic and geometric information connect immediately.

screens.title: Triangles and radians

diagram.caption: Sides a, b and c lie opposite angles A, B and C.

screens.body:
In this lesson, you will learn how to choose between the sine rule, cosine rule, and triangle area formula.

You will also explore why some triangle information can produce two different answers, before applying radian measure to arcs, sectors, and segments.
screens.keyPoint: You should know the exact sine, cosine, and tangent values at [[0^{\circ},30^{\circ},45^{\circ},60^{\circ},90^{\circ}]]. The next lesson develops them fully, but this lesson uses familiar values such as [[\sin30^{\circ}=\frac12]] and [[\sin60^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]].

screens.title: Match the rule to the information

diagram.caption: Sides a, b and c lie opposite angles A, B and C respectively.

screens.body:
Use the sine rule when you know an opposite side-angle pair:
[[\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}]].

Use the cosine rule for three sides, or two sides and their included angle:
[[a^{2}=b^{2}+c^{2}-2bc\cos A]].

Use
[[\text{Area}=\frac12bc\sin A]]
when two sides and their included angle are available.
screens.keyPoint: Do not choose a formula because it contains the letter you want. Choose it because the given information matches its structure.

screens.question: In triangle ABC, [[AB=5]], [[AC=7]], and [[\angle BAC=60^{\circ}]]. What is the exact length of BC?

diagram.caption: The known angle is included between the two known sides.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The cosine rule gives
[[BC^{2}=5^{2}+7^{2}-2(5)(7)\cos60^{\circ}]]
[[=25+49-35=39]].
Therefore [[BC=\sqrt{39}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The two known sides enclose the known angle, so use the cosine rule and the exact value [[\cos60^{\circ}=\frac12]].

screens.title: One set of data can produce two triangles
screens.question:
A triangle has [[\angle A=60^{\circ}]], [[AC=3k]], and [[BC=\sqrt7k]], where [[k>0]]. There are two possible values of AB.

What is the ratio
[[\text{larger area}:\text{smaller area}]]?

diagram.caption: The fixed information can allow two different positions for the third vertex.

steps.title: Let the unknown side be x
steps.body: Set [[AB=x]]. The side opposite the [[60^{\circ}]] angle is [[BC=\sqrt7k]].

steps.title: Compare the areas
steps.body:
Both triangles have the same side AC and included angle A. Since
[[\text{Area}=\frac12(AB)(AC)\sin A]],
their areas are in the same ratio as their possible AB lengths.

screens.finalAnswer: The larger area is twice the smaller area, so the ratio is [[2:1]].

screens.title: Why the sine rule can give two answers

diagram.caption: The same sine value can correspond to an acute angle and its obtuse supplement.

screens.body:
When side-side-angle information gives
[[\sin B=q]],
the possible angles are
[[B=\beta]]
and
[[B=180^{\circ}-\beta]],
because supplementary angles have the same sine.

The second value is valid only when it still leaves a positive third angle.
screens.keyPoint: After finding one angle, always test its supplement. Reject it if the angle sum would reach or exceed [[180^{\circ}]].

screens.question:
In triangle PQR,
[[\angle QPR=30^{\circ}]],
[[QR=a]], and [[PQ=\sqrt3a]], where [[a>0]].

Two distinct triangles are possible. What is the sum of the two possible values of PR?

diagram.caption: Use both possible angles produced by the sine rule.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The sine rule gives
[[\frac{\sin R}{\sqrt3a}=\frac{\sin30^{\circ}}{a}]],
so [[\sin R=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]]. Hence [[R=60^{\circ}]] or [[120^{\circ}]]. The corresponding Q angles are [[90^{\circ}]] and [[30^{\circ}]].

Now
[[\frac{PR}{\sin Q}=\frac{a}{\sin30^{\circ}}=2a]],
so [[PR=2a]] or [[a]]. Their sum is [[3a]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The sine rule gives two possible values for angle R. Find the remaining angle in each triangle, then calculate both possible values of PR.

screens.question:
The points [[P=(0,0,0)]], [[Q=(2,0,0)]], and [[R=(1,2,2)]] form a triangle in three-dimensional space.

What is the exact value of [[\cos\angle QPR]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The side lengths are [[PQ=2]], [[PR=3]], and [[QR=3]]. Applying the cosine rule at P:
[[3^{2}=2^{2}+3^{2}-2(2)(3)\cos\angle QPR]].
Thus [[9=13-12\cos\angle QPR]], so [[\cos\angle QPR=\frac13]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First use the three-dimensional distance formula to find the three side lengths. Then apply the cosine rule to the resulting ordinary triangle.

screens.question:
A triangle has two sides of lengths 4 and 6. Its area is [[6\sqrt3]], and the included angle between those sides is acute.

What is the length of the third side?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. From the area formula,
[[6\sqrt3=\frac12(4)(6)\sin C]],
so [[\sin C=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]]. Since C is acute, [[C=60^{\circ}]]. The cosine rule then gives
[[c^{2}=4^{2}+6^{2}-2(4)(6)\cos60^{\circ}=28]],
so [[c=2\sqrt7]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the area first to determine the included angle. The word acute tells you which angle with sine [[\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]] is required.

screens.title: Radians connect angle, arc and area

diagram.caption: The formulas work directly only when the angle is measured in radians.

screens.body:
An angle of [[\theta]] radians cuts off an arc of length
[[s=r\theta]].

The corresponding sector has area
[[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]].

Since [[\pi]] radians equals [[180^{\circ}]], convert degrees using
[[\theta_{\text{rad}}=\theta_{\text{deg}}\frac{\pi}{180}]].
screens.keyPoint: In a sector question, the same angle controls both arc length and area. This often lets you eliminate [[\theta]] rather than calculate it first.

screens.question: A sector has radius 6 and arc length [[5\pi]]. What is its exact area?

diagram.caption: Use the arc length to recover the angle, or eliminate the angle directly.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[s=r\theta]],
[[5\pi=6\theta]], so [[\theta=\frac{5\pi}{6}]]. Therefore
[[A=\frac12(6^{2})\frac{5\pi}{6}=15\pi]].

Equivalently, combine the formulas to get [[A=\frac12rs]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[s=r\theta]] and [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]]. You can also eliminate the angle to obtain [[A=\frac12rs]].

screens.title: Use one common angle
screens.question:
Two sectors are similar. Their radii are r and [[r+4]]. The smaller sector has arc length 5, and the difference between their areas is 25.

What is the positive difference between their perimeters?

diagram.caption: Similar sectors have the same central angle.

steps.title: Compare the perimeters
steps.body:
A sector perimeter is [[2r+r\theta]]. Increasing the radius by 4 changes the two straight sides by 8 and the arc by [[4\theta]].

The difference is
[[8+4\left(\frac58\right)=\frac{21}{2}]].

screens.finalAnswer: The positive difference between the perimeters is [[\frac{21}{2}]].

screens.question:
Two similar sectors have radii in the ratio [[2:3]]. The smaller arc length is [[2\pi]], and the difference between the sector areas is [[10\pi]].

What is the perimeter of the larger sector?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Let the smaller radius be r, so the larger is [[\frac32r]]. Since the smaller arc is [[2\pi]], [[r\theta=2\pi]]. The area difference is
[[\frac12\theta\left(\frac94r^{2}-r^{2}\right)=\frac58\theta r^{2}=10\pi]].
Using [[\theta r=2\pi]] gives [[\frac54\pi r=10\pi]], so [[r=8]]. The larger radius is 12 and its arc length is [[3\pi]]. Hence its perimeter is [[24+3\pi]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the common angle and the radius ratio. Arc lengths scale linearly with radius, while sector areas scale with the square of the radius.

screens.title: Subtract the central triangle

diagram.caption: A minor segment is a sector with the central triangle removed.

screens.body:
For a circle of radius r and central angle [[\theta]] radians:

sector area
[[=\frac12r^{2}\theta]],

central triangle area
[[=\frac12r^{2}\sin\theta]].

Therefore the minor-segment area is
[[\frac12r^{2}(\theta-\sin\theta)]].
screens.keyPoint: For a major segment, subtract the minor-segment area from the full circle area [[\pi r^{2}]].

screens.question:
A chord in a circle of radius 4 subtends an angle of [[\frac{\pi}{3}]] at the centre.

What is the exact area of the major segment cut off by the chord?

diagram.caption: Find the minor segment first, then subtract it from the whole circle.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The minor sector has area
[[\frac12(4^{2})\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{8\pi}{3}]],
and the central triangle has area
[[\frac12(4^{2})\sin\frac{\pi}{3}=4\sqrt3]].
So the minor segment is [[\frac{8\pi}{3}-4\sqrt3]]. Subtracting from the whole-circle area [[16\pi]] gives
[[\frac{40\pi}{3}+4\sqrt3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Calculate sector minus triangle for the minor segment. The question asks for the major segment, so subtract that result from the full circle area.

screens.title: Triangles & Radians: key points
keyPoints: Use the sine rule only when an opposite side-angle pair is available.
Use the cosine rule for SSS or SAS information.
The area formula [[\frac12ab\sin C]] can reveal an angle before another rule is used.
In an SSA problem, test both [[\theta]] and [[180^{\circ}-\theta]].
Three-dimensional problems often reduce to finding three lengths and solving an ordinary triangle.
For radians, use [[s=r\theta]] and [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]].
A segment is found by subtracting a central triangle from a sector.
reviewItems: Learn the exact trigonometric values for [[0^{\circ},30^{\circ},45^{\circ},60^{\circ},90^{\circ}]].
Redo the two-triangle questions without looking at the method.
Check that every angle used in an arc or sector formula is in radians.
Next lesson: Values, Graphs, and Identities.

screens.title: The facts that make trig equations possible

diagram.caption: On the unit circle, the horizontal coordinate is cos θ and the vertical coordinate is sin θ.

screens.body:
This lesson covers exact trigonometric values, the sine, cosine, and tangent graphs, their symmetries and periods, and the identities

[[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]]
and
[[\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}]].

The next lesson will use these facts to solve equations and count solutions.
screens.keyPoint: Most harder trigonometry questions become much quicker once the exact values and graph shapes are automatic.

screens.title: Know the five standard angles

steps.title: Cosine values
steps.body:
[[1,\frac{\sqrt3}{2},\frac{\sqrt2}{2},\frac12,0]].

These are the sine values in reverse order.

steps.title: Tangent values
steps.body:
[[0,\frac{1}{\sqrt3},1,\sqrt3,\text{undefined}]].

Use [[\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}]]. Tangent is undefined at [[90^{\circ}]] because [[\cos90^{\circ}=0]].

screens.question:
Evaluate exactly:
[[2\sin30^{\circ}\cos60^{\circ}+\tan45^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. [[\sin30^{\circ}=\frac12]], [[\cos60^{\circ}=\frac12]], and [[\tan45^{\circ}=1]]. Therefore
[[2\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac12\right)+1=\frac32]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the exact values [[\sin30^{\circ}=\frac12]], [[\cos60^{\circ}=\frac12]], and [[\tan45^{\circ}=1]].

screens.title: Same range and period, different starting points

diagram.caption: Sine begins at 0; cosine begins at 1. Both repeat every 2π.

screens.body:
Both [[y=\sin x]] and [[y=\cos x]] have range
[[-1\leq y\leq1]]
and period [[2\pi]].

For sine, the key values over one cycle are
[[\sin0=0]], [[\sin\frac{\pi}{2}=1]], [[\sin\pi=0]], [[\sin\frac{3\pi}{2}=-1]], and [[\sin2\pi=0]].

Cosine follows the same shape but begins at 1: [[\cos0=1]].
screens.keyPoint: Cosine is the sine graph shifted left by [[\frac{\pi}{2}]]: [[\cos x=\sin\left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)]].

screens.title: Use the graph instead of memorising every angle
screens.body:
Sine and cosine repeat every [[2\pi]]:
[[\sin(x+2\pi)=\sin x]],
[[\cos(x+2\pi)=\cos x]].

Sine is odd:
[[\sin(-x)=-\sin x]].
Its graph has rotational symmetry about the origin.

Cosine is even:
[[\cos(-x)=\cos x]].
Its graph has reflection symmetry in the y-axis.
screens.keyPoint: Useful within one cycle: [[\sin(\pi-x)=\sin x]] and [[\cos(2\pi-x)=\cos x]].

screens.question:
Find the exact value of
[[\sin\left(\frac{13\pi}{6}\right)+\cos\left(\frac{5\pi}{3}\right)]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Subtracting [[2\pi]] gives
[[\sin\frac{13\pi}{6}=\sin\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac12]].
Also [[\frac{5\pi}{3}=2\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}]], so
[[\cos\frac{5\pi}{3}=\cos\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac12]].
The total is 1.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use periodicity on [[\frac{13\pi}{6}]] and the symmetry [[\cos(2\pi-x)=\cos x]] on [[\frac{5\pi}{3}]].

screens.title: Tangent repeats sooner and has asymptotes
screens.body:
The tangent graph has period [[\pi]]:
[[\tan(x+\pi)=\tan x]].

It crosses the x-axis at
[[x=n\pi]],
and is undefined where [[\cos x=0]], giving vertical asymptotes at
[[x=\frac{\pi}{2}+n\pi]],
where n is an integer.

Tangent has range all real numbers and is odd:
[[\tan(-x)=-\tan x]].
screens.keyPoint: Because [[\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}]], every zero of cosine is a point where tangent is undefined.

screens.question:
How many vertical asymptotes does [[y=\tan x]] have in the interval
[[-2\pi
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The asymptotes are
[[-\frac{3\pi}{2},-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{3\pi}{2}]],
so there are four.
screens.incorrectFeedback: List values of [[x=\frac{\pi}{2}+n\pi]] strictly between [[-2\pi]] and [[2\pi]].

screens.title: You can now read the three basic trig graphs.
screens.body:
You know their ranges, periods, symmetries, zeros, and the tangent asymptotes.

Next, we use the two identities from the specification to simplify expressions and recover missing values.

screens.title: Two identities connect all three functions
screens.body:
An identity is true for every value for which both sides are defined.

The Pythagorean identity is
[[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]].

Useful rearrangements are
[[\sin^{2}x=1-\cos^{2}x]]
and
[[\cos^{2}x=1-\sin^{2}x]].

The quotient identity is
[[\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}]],
provided [[\cos x\neq0]].
screens.keyPoint: Remember that [[\sin^{2}x]] means [[(\sin x)^{2}]], not [[\sin(x^{2})]].

screens.question:
Simplify, where the expression is defined:
[[\frac{1-\cos^{2}x}{\sin x\cos x}]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[1-\cos^{2}x=\sin^{2}x]],
[[\frac{1-\cos^{2}x}{\sin x\cos x}=\frac{\sin^{2}x}{\sin x\cos x}=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}=\tan x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Replace [[1-\cos^{2}x]] with [[\sin^{2}x]], cancel one factor of sine, then use the quotient identity.

screens.title: A squared identity gives two possible signs
screens.question: Suppose [[\sin x=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]] and [[\cos x<0]]. What is [[\tan x]]?
screens.finalAnswer: The correct answer is A: [[\tan x=-\sqrt3]].

screens.question:
What is the smallest positive period of
[[f(x)=\sin^{2}x]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[\sin(x+\pi)=-\sin x]], squaring removes the sign:
[[\sin^{2}(x+\pi)=(-\sin x)^{2}=\sin^{2}x]].
Therefore the smallest positive period is [[\pi]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Although sine has period [[2\pi]], shifting by [[\pi]] only changes its sign. Squaring removes that change.

screens.title: Values, Graphs and Identities: key points
keyPoints: Know the exact sine, cosine, and tangent values at [[0^{\circ},30^{\circ},45^{\circ},60^{\circ},90^{\circ}]].
Sine and cosine have range [[-1\leq y\leq1]] and period [[2\pi]].
Sine and tangent are odd; cosine is even.
Tangent has period [[\pi]] and asymptotes at [[x=\frac{\pi}{2}+n\pi]].
Use [[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]] to replace squared sine or cosine terms.
Use [[\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}]] only when [[\cos x\neq0]].
reviewItems: Memorise the standard exact-value table in both degrees and radians.
Sketch each basic graph from its key points rather than from memory alone.
Check signs when taking a square root from [[\sin^{2}x]] or [[\cos^{2}x]].
Next lesson: Solving Trigonometric Equations.

screens.title: Find the value, identify every matching angle, then filter the interval
screens.body:
For an equation such as [[\sin x=k]], first find the reference angle.

Then identify every angle where the graph has the required value. Finally, use periodicity to list all solutions in the stated interval. The interval is part of the question, not an afterthought.
screens.keyPoint: Find the reference angle, identify every matching angle, then filter the interval.

diagram.caption: The horizontal line y = k meets the sine graph at two angles in one cycle.

screens.title: Solve [[\sin x=\frac12]] for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]

steps.title: Choose the quadrants
steps.body: Sine is positive in quadrants I and II.

diagram.caption: For sin x = ½, the two solutions are x = π⁄6 and x = 5π⁄6.

screens.question: Solve [[\cos x=-\frac{\sqrt2}{2}]] for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The reference angle is [[\frac{\pi}{4}]], and cosine is negative in quadrants II and III.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use reference angle [[\frac{\pi}{4}]], then choose the two quadrants where the x-coordinate is negative.

screens.title: Tangent repeats every [[\pi]]
screens.body:
If [[\tan x=k]] has one solution [[x=\alpha]], then its general solution is [[x=\alpha+n\pi]], where [[n]] is an integer.

This single family captures the solutions in quadrants I and III, or II and IV, because opposite points on the unit circle have the same tangent ratio.
screens.keyPoint: When the argument is [[bx+c]], solve for the whole angle first, then isolate [[x]].

screens.title: Transform the interval with the angle
screens.question: Solve [[\tan(2x)=-1]] for [[-\frac{\pi}{2}\lt x\lt\frac{\pi}{2}]].

steps.title: Solve for the new angle
steps.body: Within this interval, [[\tan u=-1]] at [[u=-\frac{\pi}{4}]] and [[u=\frac{3\pi}{4}]].

steps.title: Return to x
steps.body: Divide both values by [[2]] to obtain [[x=-\frac{\pi}{8}]] and [[x=\frac{3\pi}{8}]].

screens.question: How many solutions does [[\cos(2x)=0]] have in [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[2x=\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{3\pi}{2},\frac{5\pi}{2},\frac{7\pi}{2}]], giving four values of [[x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Transform the interval to [[0\le2x\lt4\pi]] and count every cosine zero.

screens.title: You can solve direct equations over any interval.
screens.body: Next, identities turn disguised equations into algebraic ones.

screens.title: Treat one trigonometric function as the variable
screens.body:
An equation such as [[2\sin^{2}x-\sin x-1=0]] is a quadratic in [[\sin x]]. Factor or solve it exactly, then solve each resulting trigonometric equation.

If both sine and cosine appear, use [[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]] to rewrite everything in one function.
screens.keyPoint: Reject algebraic roots outside [[-1\le\sin x\le1]] or [[-1\le\cos x\le1]].

screens.title: Convert cosine squared into sine
screens.question: Solve [[2\cos^{2}x-3\sin x=0]] for [[0^{\circ}\le x\lt360^{\circ}]].

steps.title: Factor the quadratic
steps.body: Rearranging gives [[2\sin^{2}x+3\sin x-2=0]], so [[(2\sin x-1)(\sin x+2)=0]].

steps.title: Keep valid function values
steps.body: [[\sin x=-2]] is impossible. From [[\sin x=\frac12]], the interval gives [[x=30^{\circ},150^{\circ}]].

screens.question: Solve [[2\sin^{2}x-\sin x-1=0]] for [[0^{\circ}\le x\lt360^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[(2\sin x+1)(\sin x-1)=0]], so [[\sin x=-\frac12]] or [[\sin x=1]]. The interval gives [[90^{\circ},210^{\circ},330^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor as a quadratic in [[\sin x]], then solve both valid sine equations.

screens.title: Endpoints and transformed intervals decide the final list
screens.body:
Distinguish [[a\lt x\lt b]] from [[a\le x\le b]]. An endpoint may duplicate a periodic value, but it is included only when the inequality allows it.

For [[f(bx+c)=k]], either transform the interval for [[u=bx+c]] or generate a general solution before filtering.
screens.keyPoint: Substitute each final candidate into the original equation, especially after squaring, dividing, or changing variables.

screens.title: Preserve every valid branch
screens.question: Solve [[12\cos^{2}x+6\sin x-10=2]] for [[0^{\circ}\lt x\lt360^{\circ}]].

steps.title: Filter the open interval
steps.body: [[\sin x=0]] gives [[180^{\circ}]] because [[0^{\circ}]] and [[360^{\circ}]] are excluded. [[\sin x=\frac12]] gives [[30^{\circ},150^{\circ}]].

screens.question: How many solutions does [[\sin^{2}\left(2x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac12]] have for [[-2\pi\lt x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\sin^{2}u=\frac12]] repeats every [[\frac{\pi}{2}]] in [[u]]. With [[u=2x+\frac{\pi}{3}]], the solutions in the transformed interval correspond to sixteen integer values.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[u=\frac{\pi}{4}+k\frac{\pi}{2}]], substitute [[u=2x+\frac{\pi}{3}]], then count integers satisfying the x-interval.

screens.title: Solving Trigonometric Equations: key points
keyPoints: Find a reference angle, choose quadrants, then filter the interval.
Use periods [[2\pi]] for sine and cosine and [[\pi]] for tangent.
Transform the interval when the argument is [[bx+c]].
Treat equations in [[\sin x]] or [[\cos x]] as ordinary quadratics.
Use [[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]] to reduce mixed equations to one function.
reviewItems: List every solution, not only the inverse-function output.
Respect open and closed endpoints.
Reject impossible sine or cosine values.
Check solutions in the original equation.
Count periodic solutions algebraically on long intervals.

screens.question: Two sides of a triangle have lengths [[7]] and [[9]], and the angle between them is [[60^{\circ}]]. What is the exact length of the third side?

diagram.caption: The known angle is included between the two known sides.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. By the cosine rule,
[[c^{2}=7^{2}+9^{2}-2(7)(9)\cos60^{\circ}]]
[[=49+81-63=67]].
Therefore [[c=\sqrt{67}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the cosine rule with the two known sides and their included angle.

screens.question: In triangle [[ABC]], [[A=30^{\circ}]], [[a=5]], and [[b=8]]. How many distinct triangles satisfy this information?

diagram.caption: Side-side-angle information may produce two different triangles.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The sine rule gives
[[\sin B=\frac{8\sin30^{\circ}}{5}=\frac45]].
This gives an acute value of B and its obtuse supplement. Both leave a positive value for angle C, so two triangles are possible.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find [[\sin B]], then test both the inverse-sine angle and its supplement.

screens.question: A triangle has two sides of lengths [[8]] and [[10]], and its area is [[20\sqrt3]]. Let the two possible lengths of the third side be [[p]] and [[q]], where [[p\lt q]]. What is [[q^{2}-p^{2}]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. From
[[20\sqrt3=\frac12(8)(10)\sin C]],
we get [[\sin C=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]], so [[C=60^{\circ}]] or [[120^{\circ}]].

The cosine rule gives
[[p^{2}=8^{2}+10^{2}-160\cos60^{\circ}=84]]
and
[[q^{2}=8^{2}+10^{2}-160\cos120^{\circ}=244]].
Hence [[q^{2}-p^{2}=160]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the area formula to find both possible included angles, then apply the cosine rule to each.

screens.question: A cuboid has side lengths [[6]], [[8]], and [[24]]. Let [[\theta]] be the angle between its space diagonal and its [[6\times8]] base. Find [[\cos\theta]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The diagonal of the base has length
[[\sqrt{6^{2}+8^{2}}=10]],
while the space diagonal has length
[[\sqrt{10^{2}+24^{2}}=26]].
Therefore [[\cos\theta=\frac{10}{26}=\frac5{13}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First find the base diagonal. It is the projection of the space diagonal onto the base.

screens.question: A sector has arc length [[8\pi]] and area [[48\pi]]. What is its perimeter?

diagram.caption: The same radius and angle determine both arc length and sector area.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[s=r\theta]] and [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]], we have
[[A=\frac12rs]].
Thus
[[48\pi=\frac12r(8\pi)]],
so [[r=12]]. The perimeter is
[[2r+s=24+8\pi]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Eliminate the angle from the arc-length and sector-area formulas to obtain [[A=\frac12rs]].

screens.question: A chord of a circle of radius [[6]] subtends an angle of [[\frac{2\pi}{3}]] at the centre. What is the exact area of the major segment?

diagram.caption: Find the minor segment first, then subtract it from the full circle.

screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The minor sector area is
[[\frac12(6^{2})\frac{2\pi}{3}=12\pi]],
and the central triangle area is
[[\frac12(6^{2})\sin\frac{2\pi}{3}=9\sqrt3]].

The minor segment has area [[12\pi-9\sqrt3]]. Subtracting this from [[36\pi]] gives
[[24\pi+9\sqrt3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Calculate sector minus triangle for the minor segment, then subtract that result from the full circle.

screens.question:
Evaluate exactly:
[[2\sin\frac{5\pi}{6}-\sqrt3\cos\frac{2\pi}{3}+\tan\frac{7\pi}{4}]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The three terms are
[[2\sin\frac{5\pi}{6}=1]],
[[-\sqrt3\cos\frac{2\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]],
and
[[\tan\frac{7\pi}{4}=-1]].
The total is [[\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the reference angles and pay careful attention to the signs in quadrants II and IV.

screens.question:
What is the smallest positive period of
[[f(x)=\sin^{2}(4x)]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The function [[\sin^{2}u]] has period [[\pi]]. Replacing [[u]] by [[4x]] divides the period by 4, giving [[\frac{\pi}{4}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Squaring sine reduces its period to [[\pi]], and the factor 4 compresses the graph horizontally.

screens.question:
How many solutions does
[[\sin(3x)=\frac12]]
have for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The transformed interval is
[[0\le3x\lt6\pi]],
which contains three complete sine cycles. There are two solutions in each cycle, giving six solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Transform the whole interval for [[3x]] before counting the solutions.

screens.question:
How many solutions does
[[\tan\left(2x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=1]]
have for [[-\pi\lt x\lt\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since
[[\tan u=1]] when [[u=\frac{\pi}{4}+n\pi]],
we obtain
[[2x+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}+n\pi]],
so [[x=\frac{n\pi}{2}]].
The values strictly between [[-\pi]] and [[\pi]] are
[[-\frac{\pi}{2},0,\frac{\pi}{2}]],
giving three solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the general tangent solution, isolate x, then filter using the open interval.

screens.question:
For [[0\le x\lt2\pi]], the equation
[[2\cos^{2}x+\cos x-1=0]]
has [[n]] solutions whose sum is [[S]]. Which pair [[(n,S)]] is correct?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Factorising gives
[[(2\cos x-1)(\cos x+1)=0]].
Thus
[[x=\frac{\pi}{3},\pi,\frac{5\pi}{3}]].
There are three solutions and their sum is [[3\pi]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Treat cosine as an algebraic variable, then solve both resulting cosine equations.

screens.question:
The angle [[x]] satisfies
[[\sin2x+\sqrt3\cos2x=1]]
and
[[\sqrt3\sin2x-\cos2x=-\sqrt3]],
where [[0^{\circ}\lt x\lt360^{\circ}]].

What is the sum of the possible values of [[x]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Solving the simultaneous equations gives
[[\sin2x=-\frac12]]
and
[[\cos2x=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]].
Therefore
[[2x=330^{\circ}+360^{\circ}n]],
so
[[x=165^{\circ}+180^{\circ}n]].
The valid values are [[165^{\circ}]] and [[345^{\circ}]], whose sum is [[510^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Treat [[\sin2x]] and [[\cos2x]] as two algebraic unknowns and solve the simultaneous equations first.

screens.question:
What fraction of the interval [[0\le\theta\le\pi]] satisfies
[[\left(\sin2\theta-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\right)(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)\ge0]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The first factor is non-negative for
[[\frac{\pi}{6}\le\theta\le\frac{\pi}{3}]],
while [[\sin\theta+\cos\theta]] changes sign at [[\frac{3\pi}{4}]].

The product is non-negative when
[[\frac{\pi}{6}\le\theta\le\frac{\pi}{3}]]
and when
[[\frac{3\pi}{4}\le\theta\le\pi]].

The total length is
[[\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{5\pi}{12}]],
which is [[\frac5{12}]] of the full interval.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find every zero of both factors, place them in order on the interval, then use a sign chart.

screens.question:
How many real solutions are there to
[[4\cos x=\sqrt{x}]],
where [[x]] is measured in radians?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[\sqrt{x}\ge0]], solutions can occur only where [[\cos x\ge0]]. Also, [[\sqrt{x}\le4]] requires [[0\le x\le16]].

On this restricted domain, the graphs [[y=4\cos x]] and [[y=\sqrt{x}]] intersect once in the first positive-cosine section and twice in each of the next two positive-cosine sections. Therefore there are five real solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback: First use [[\sqrt{x}\le4]] to restrict the domain to [[0\le x\le16]]. Then count intersections only where cosine is non-negative.

screens.question:
For [[0^{\circ}\le x\le180^{\circ}]], the equation
[[2-3\cos^{2}x=|\cos x|]]
has [[n]] solutions whose sum is [[S]]. Which pair [[(n,S)]] is correct?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Let [[u=|\cos x|]]. Then
[[2-3u^{2}=u]],
so
[[3u^{2}+u-2=0]].
This gives [[u=\frac23]] or [[u=-1]], and the negative value is rejected.

The equation [[|\cos x|=\frac23]] has two solutions in the interval. They are symmetric about [[90^{\circ}]], so their sum is [[180^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute [[u=|\cos x|]], solve the quadratic, reject invalid values, then use cosine symmetry.

screens.question:
How many solutions does
[[\sin2x+\sin^{2}x=1]]
have for [[0\le x\le2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Using [[\sin2x=2\sin x\cos x]] and [[1-\sin^{2}x=\cos^{2}x]] gives
[[2\sin x\cos x=\cos^{2}x]].
Hence
[[\cos x(2\sin x-\cos x)=0]].

The equation [[\cos x=0]] gives two solutions, and [[\tan x=\frac12]] gives two more, for four in total.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite both sides so that the equation can be factorised. Do not divide by cosine, as that would lose solutions.

screens.question:
How many solutions does
[[\cos(3x)\ln x=\sin(3x)]]
have for [[0\lt x\le2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. At any solution, [[\cos3x\neq0]], so the equation may be written as
[[\tan3x=\ln x]].

Over [[0\lt x\le2\pi]], compare the slowly increasing logarithm with the separate increasing branches of [[\tan3x]]. There is one intersection on each of five valid branches, giving five solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the domain [[x>0]], rewrite the equation as [[\tan3x=\ln x]], and count the intersections on each tangent branch up to [[2\pi]].

screens.question:
How many solutions does
[[(1+2\cos3x)^{2}=9]]
have for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Taking both square-root branches gives
[[1+2\cos3x=3]]
or
[[1+2\cos3x=-3]].
The second branch requires [[\cos3x=-2]], which is impossible. Therefore [[\cos3x=1]].

Within the interval, this occurs at
[[x=0,\frac{2\pi}{3},\frac{4\pi}{3}]],
giving three solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Split the squared equation into both positive and negative branches, then reject impossible cosine values.

screens.question:
For [[0\le x\lt2\pi]], the equation
[[\cos2x=\sin x]]
has [[n]] solutions whose sum is [[S]]. Which pair [[(n,S)]] is correct?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Using [[\cos2x=1-2\sin^{2}x]] gives
[[1-2\sin^{2}x=\sin x]],
so
[[(2\sin x-1)(\sin x+1)=0]].

Thus
[[x=\frac{\pi}{6},\frac{5\pi}{6},\frac{3\pi}{2}]].
There are three solutions and their sum is [[2\pi]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Express cosine of the double angle entirely in terms of sine, then factor the resulting quadratic.

screens.question:
How many solutions does
[[\frac{3^{\tan^{2}x}}{27^{\sin^{2}x}}=1]]
have for [[0\le x\le2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[27=3^{3}]], equality of the positive powers gives
[[\tan^{2}x=3\sin^{2}x]].

If [[\sin x=0]], then
[[x=0,\pi,2\pi]],
giving three solutions.

Otherwise, dividing by [[\sin^{2}x]] gives
[[\frac1{\cos^{2}x}=3]],
so [[\cos^{2}x=\frac13]], which gives four further solutions. The total is seven.
screens.incorrectFeedback: First equate the powers of 3. Keep the separate case [[\sin x=0]] before dividing by [[\sin^{2}x]].

screens.title: Trigonometry: exam review
keyPoints: Choose the sine rule, cosine rule, or area formula from the information given.
Check the supplementary solution in angle-side-side triangle problems.
Use radians for arc length, sector area, and segment area.
Connect exact values, quadrants, graph symmetry, and periodicity.
Solve transformed and quadratic trigonometric equations over the full interval.
reviewItems: Label opposite side-angle pairs before calculating.
Keep exact values instead of rounding.
Check whether interval endpoints are included.
Reject sine or cosine values outside [[-1\le y\le1]].
Repeat this set until every periodic solution is found systematically.

screens.title: A logarithm is an exponent
screens.body:
The statements [[a^{b}=c]] and [[\log_{a}c=b]] say exactly the same thing.

For example, [[2^{5}=32]], so [[\log_{2}32=5]]. A logarithm answers the question: what power of the base produces this number?
screens.keyPoint: For real logarithms, the base must satisfy [[a>0]] and [[a\ne1]]. The logarithm's argument must also be positive.

screens.title: Move between exponential and logarithmic form

screens.question: What is [[\log_{4}64]]?
screens.prompt: Ask which power of 4 gives 64.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite the question as [[4^{x}=64]]. Since [[4^{3}=64]], the logarithm is 3.

screens.title: Products add, quotients subtract and powers come down
screens.body:
For positive x and y:

[[\log_{a}x+\log_{a}y=\log_{a}(xy)]].

[[\log_{a}x-\log_{a}y=\log_{a}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)]].

[[k\log_{a}x=\log_{a}(x^{k})]].
screens.keyPoint: The logarithms must have the same base before the product or quotient laws can be used.

screens.title: Log laws come directly from exponent laws

steps.title: Represent x and y as powers

screens.title: Useful special cases
screens.body:
Because [[a^{1}=a]], we have [[\log_{a}a=1]].

Because [[a^{0}=1]], we have [[\log_{a}1=0]].

Because [[\frac{1}{x}=x^{-1}]], the power law gives [[\log_{a}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=-\log_{a}x]].
screens.keyPoint: The reciprocal result follows from the power law with exponent [[-1]]. Remember that the logarithm's argument must be positive.

screens.title: Separate products, quotients and powers

steps.title: Use the quotient and product laws

screens.title: Combine several logarithms into one

screens.question: Evaluate [[3\log_{2}4-\log_{2}8]].
screens.prompt: Combine the logarithms before evaluating.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the power law first: [[3\log_{2}4=\log_{2}(4^{3})]]. Then use the quotient law.

screens.title: The graph of [[y=a^{x}]]
screens.body:
For [[a>0]] and [[a\ne1]], the graph of [[y=a^{x}]] is defined for every real value of x.

Since [[a^{0}=1]], every exponential graph passes through [[(0,1)]]. Its outputs are always positive, so it never crosses or touches the x-axis.
screens.keyPoint: The domain is all real x, the range is [[y>0]], and the x-axis is the horizontal asymptote [[y=0]].

screens.question: Which statement is true about [[y=\left(\frac14\right)^{x}]]?
options: It decreases and crosses the x-axis.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Because [[0<\frac14<1]], the graph decreases. Like every exponential graph, it passes through [[(0,1)]] and has range [[y>0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A positive exponential never reaches the x-axis. Since the base lies between 0 and 1, the graph decreases and passes through [[(0,1)]].

screens.title: Log Laws & Exponential Graphs: key points
keyPoints: [[a^{b}=c]] is equivalent to [[\log_{a}c=b]].
Logarithms of products add, while logarithms of quotients subtract.
The power law gives [[\log_{a}(x^{k})=k\log_{a}x]].
[[\log_{a}a=1]], [[\log_{a}1=0]] and [[\log_{a}\left(\frac1x\right)=-\log_{a}x]].
[[y=a^{x}]] passes through [[(0,1)]], has range [[y>0]] and has horizontal asymptote [[y=0]].
reviewItems: Practise switching between exponential and logarithmic form.
Practise expanding a single logarithm.
Practise condensing several logarithms into one.
Remember that every logarithm argument must be positive.
Next lesson: Solving Exponential and Log Equations.

screens.title: Simplify before choosing a method
screens.body:
An exponential equation may initially look complicated, but the aim is usually to reduce it to the form [[a^{x}=b]].

First simplify powers and isolate the exponential expression. If both sides can then be written using the same base, equate their exponents. Otherwise, use the definition of a logarithm.
screens.keyPoint: Do not take logarithms too early. Look first for common bases, factors and repeated powers.

steps.title: Equate the exponents
steps.body: Since the bases are the same, [[3x+3=4x-2]].

screens.question: Solve [[27^{2x-1}=9^{x+2}]].
screens.prompt: Write both sides as powers of 3.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The equation becomes [[3^{6x-3}=3^{2x+4}]]. Therefore [[6x-3=2x+4]], giving [[x=\frac{7}{4}]].

screens.title: The bases will not always match
screens.body:
If an equation has been reduced to [[a^{x}=b]], then its solution can be written as [[x=\log_{a}b]].

For example, [[5^{2x-1}=7]] gives [[2x-1=\log_{5}7]], so [[x=\frac{1+\log_{5}7}{2}]].
screens.keyPoint: An exact logarithmic answer is acceptable. The change-of-base formula is not required.

steps.title: Undo the subtraction
steps.body: Add 5 to both sides to get [[3(2^{x+1})=24]].

screens.question: Solve [[4(3^{2x})-7=13]], giving your answer exactly.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Rearranging gives [[3^{2x}=5]], so [[2x=\log_{3}5]] and [[x=\frac{1}{2}\log_{3}5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First isolate the power: [[4(3^{2x})=20]], so [[3^{2x}=5]]. The exponent is [[2x]], not x.

screens.title: Factor out a common exponential
screens.body:
Terms such as [[a^{x+1}]] and [[a^{x}]] share a common factor because [[a^{x+1}=a\cdot a^{x}]].

For example:

[[5^{x+1}-2(5^{x})=375]]
[[5^{x}(5-2)=375]]
[[3(5^{x})=375]]
[[5^{x}=125]], so [[x=3]].
screens.keyPoint: Treat [[a^{x}]] like an algebraic factor. Factoring may reveal a much simpler exponential equation.

screens.question: Solve [[2^{x+2}+2^{x}=80]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Factorising gives [[2^{x}(2^{2}+1)=80]], so [[5(2^{x})=80]]. Therefore [[2^{x}=16]] and [[x=4]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[2^{x+2}=2^{x}\cdot2^{2}]]. Both terms then contain the factor [[2^{x}]].

screens.title: Some exponential equations hide a quadratic
screens.body:
Since [[a^{2x}=(a^{x})^{2}]], an equation containing both [[a^{2x}]] and [[a^{x}]] may be quadratic in disguise.

Let [[u=a^{x}]]. Solve the resulting quadratic, then substitute back.
screens.keyPoint: Because [[a^{x}>0]], the substituted variable must satisfy [[u>0]]. Reject any zero or negative value of u.

screens.title: Expose the hidden quadratic
screens.question: Solve [[16^{x}-17(4^{x})+16=0]].
screens.finalAnswer: The solutions are [[x=0]] and [[x=2]], so the correct option is C.

screens.question: Solve [[9^{x}+2(3^{x})-8=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: After using [[u=3^{x}]], remember that u must be positive. One root of the quadratic is therefore impossible.

screens.title: Combine, convert and check
screens.body:
When solving a logarithmic equation, first state the domain restrictions. Then use the log laws to combine terms and convert the result into exponential form.

Algebra may produce a value that makes an original logarithm undefined, so each candidate must be checked.
screens.keyPoint: Every logarithm argument must be strictly positive. A solution of the rearranged equation is not automatically valid.

screens.title: Use the original domain to reject a root
screens.question: Solve [[\log_{2}(x-2)+\log_{2}(x-5)=2]].
screens.finalAnswer: The only valid solution is [[x=6]], so the correct option is B.

screens.question: The real numbers x and y satisfy [[3^{2x}=9^{y+1}]] and [[27^{x-1}=3^{y+4}]]. What is [[x+y]]?
screens.prompt: Rewrite every term using base 3.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The first equation gives [[2x=2y+2]], so [[x-y=1]]. The second gives [[3x-3=y+4]], so [[3x-y=7]]. Solving simultaneously gives [[x=3]] and [[y=2]], hence [[x+y=5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Convert [[9^{y+1}]] to [[3^{2y+2}]] and [[27^{x-1}]] to [[3^{3x-3}]]. This produces two linear equations in x and y.

screens.title: Solving Exponential and Log Equations: key points
keyPoints: Simplify and isolate an exponential expression before choosing a method.
When both sides have the same base, equate their exponents.
The equation [[a^{x}=b]] can be written as [[x=\log_{a}b]].
Factor common exponential terms or use [[u=a^{x}>0]] when a quadratic is hidden.
For logarithmic equations, combine logs and check every answer against the original domain.
reviewItems: Practise recognising useful common bases.
Practise isolating an exponential before converting to logarithmic form.
Look for a common factor when powers have related exponents.
Reject non-positive values introduced by exponential substitution.
Next lesson: Exponentials and Logarithms Exam-style Questions.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\log_{3}\left(\frac{1}{81}\right)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[\frac{1}{81}=3^{-4}]], we have [[\log_{3}\left(\frac{1}{81}\right)=-4]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write the argument as a power of 3. Because [[81=3^{4}]], its reciprocal is [[3^{-4}]].

screens.question: Solve [[3^{x+1}+3^{x-1}=10(2^{x})]], giving your answer exactly.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Factor the left side: [[3^{x-1}(3^{2}+1)=10(2^{x})]]. Therefore [[10(3^{x-1})=10(2^{x})]], so [[3^{x-1}=2^{x}]]. This gives [[\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{x}=3]], hence [[x=\log_{\frac{3}{2}}3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor out [[3^{x-1}]] first. After cancelling 10, rearrange the equation into the form [[\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{x}=3]].

screens.question: Given [[\log_{a}b=4]], find [[\log_{a}\left(\frac{a^{7}}{b^{3/2}}\right)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The log laws give [[7-\frac{3}{2}\log_{a}b=7-\frac{3}{2}(4)=1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the quotient and power laws. The expression becomes [[7-\frac{3}{2}\log_{a}b]].

screens.question: For which values of b does [[a^{x}=b]] have exactly one real solution, where [[a>0]] and [[a\ne1]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The range of [[y=a^{x}]] is [[y>0]], and the function is one-to-one, so every positive b gives exactly one real solution.
screens.incorrectFeedback: An exponential output is always positive. The graph never reaches zero or takes a negative value.

screens.question: For [[x>3]], find x if [[\log_{2}(x^{2}-9)-\log_{2}(x-3)]] = [[\log_{2}(3x-1)-1]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[x^{2}-9=(x-3)(x+3)]], the left side becomes [[\log_{2}(x+3)]]. Also, [[1=\log_{2}2]], so the right side is [[\log_{2}\left(\frac{3x-1}{2}\right)]]. Therefore [[x+3=\frac{3x-1}{2}]], giving [[x=7]], which satisfies [[x>3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor [[x^{2}-9]] as a difference of two squares. Also rewrite [[1]] as [[\log_{2}2]] so that the right side can be condensed into one logarithm.

screens.question: Solve [[5(3^{2x+1})=40]], giving your answer exactly.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. First [[3^{2x+1}=8]], so [[2x+1=\log_{3}8]]. Therefore [[x=\frac{\log_{3}8-1}{2}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide by 5 first. The exponent is [[2x+1]], so after converting to logarithmic form you must still rearrange for x.

screens.question: The equation [[2^{x}+8^{x}=18(4^{x})]] has two real solutions p and q. Find [[p+q]].
screens.prompt: You do not need to calculate the two solutions separately.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Divide through by [[4^{x}]] to get [[2^{-x}+2^{x}=18]]. Let [[u=2^{x}>0]]. Then [[u+\frac{1}{u}=18]], so [[u^{2}-18u+1=0]]. The two u-values have product 1. Therefore [[2^{p}2^{q}=1]], so [[2^{p+q}=1]] and [[p+q=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide every term by [[4^{x}]], then let [[u=2^{x}]]. Use the product of the quadratic's roots rather than solving the quadratic completely.

screens.question: Solve [[\frac{5^{x+2}}{25^{x-1}}=125]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The left side is [[5^{x+2-(2x-2)}=5^{4-x}]]. Since [[125=5^{3}]], we get [[4-x=3]], so [[x=1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite [[25^{x-1}]] as [[5^{2x-2}]], then subtract the denominator's exponent from the numerator's exponent.

screens.question: Solve [[25^{x}-26(5^{x})+25=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[25^{x}=(5^{x})^{2}]]. The resulting quadratic factorises using 1 and 25.

screens.question: Solve [[4^{x}+3(2^{x})-10=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: After solving the quadratic in [[u=2^{x}]], reject any non-positive value because an exponential is always positive.

screens.question: For [[x>7]], solve [[\log_{3}((x-1)(x-7))=2]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Convert to exponential form: [[(x-1)(x-7)=9]]. This gives [[x^{2}-8x-2=0]], so [[x=4\pm3\sqrt{2}]]. Only [[x=4+3\sqrt{2}]] satisfies [[x>7]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Convert the logarithmic equation into [[(x-1)(x-7)=9]], solve the resulting quadratic and then apply the restriction [[x>7]].

screens.question: For [[x>0]], solve [[3\log_{2}(2x)-\log_{2}(4x^{2})=5]].
screens.prompt: Rewrite every variable term as a multiple of [[\log_{2}x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[\log_{2}(2x)=1+\log_{2}x]] and [[\log_{2}(4x^{2})=2+2\log_{2}x]]. Then collect the multiples of [[\log_{2}x]].

screens.question: For [[x>0]], solve [[(\log_{3}(9x))^{2}-(\log_{3}x)^{2}=20]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Recognise a difference of two squares. The difference between the two logarithms is simply [[\log_{3}9=2]].

screens.question: Find the positive difference between the two real values of x satisfying [[(\log_{2}x)^{2}+3\log_{2}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)-4=0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let [[t=\log_{2}x]]. Since [[\log_{2}(1/x)=-t]], we get [[t^{2}-3t-4=0]], so [[t=4]] or [[t=-1]]. Thus [[x=16]] or [[x=\frac12]], whose positive difference is [[\frac{31}{2}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Replace [[\log_{2}(1/x)]] with [[-\log_{2}x]], then solve the quadratic in [[t=\log_{2}x]]. Remember to convert both t-values back to x.

screens.question: Positive x satisfies [[2\log_{3}x=\log_{3}(9x-18)]]. What is the sum of all possible values of x?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The equation becomes [[\log_{3}(x^{2})=\log_{3}(9x-18)]], so [[x^{2}=9x-18]]. The roots are [[x=3]] and [[x=6]], both valid, and their sum is 9.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the power law, then equate the logarithm arguments. Check that each root makes [[9x-18]] positive.

screens.question: Find the minimum value of [[3^{2x}-8(3^{x})+19]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let [[u=3^{x}>0]]. Then [[u^{2}-8u+19=(u-4)^{2}+3]], whose minimum value is 3. The value [[u=4]] is possible because [[3^{x}]] can equal any positive number.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Treat [[3^{x}]] as one positive variable and complete the square in that variable.

screens.question: For which values of k does [[4^{x}-5(2^{x})+k=0]] have exactly one real solution for x?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. With [[u=2^{x}>0]], the equation is [[u^{2}-5u+k=0]]. If [[k\le0]], exactly one root is positive because the other is non-positive. If [[k=\frac{25}{4}]], there is one repeated positive root. Other positive k-values give either two positive roots or none.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Work with [[u=2^{x}>0]]. Count positive roots of [[u^{2}-5u+k=0]], remembering that [[u=0]] is not allowed.

screens.question: Real numbers x and y satisfy [[2^{3x}=4^{y+2}]] and [[8^{x-1}=2^{y+5}]]. What is [[x+y]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Writing everything in base 2 gives [[3x=2y+4]] and [[3x-3=y+5]]. Thus [[3x-2y=4]] and [[3x-y=8]], giving [[y=4]] and [[x=4]]. Therefore [[x+y=8]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Convert 4 and 8 to powers of 2. You will obtain two simultaneous linear equations in x and y.

screens.question: Solve [[\frac{4^{x}-1}{2^{x}-1}=5]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let [[u=2^{x}]], noting that [[u\ne1]] because the original denominator cannot be zero. Then [[\frac{u^{2}-1}{u-1}=u+1=5]], so [[u=4]] and [[x=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[4^{x}=(2^{x})^{2}]] and factor the numerator as a difference of two squares. Also exclude [[x=0]] because it makes the denominator zero.

screens.question: The equation [[2^{(\log_{2}x)^{2}}=x^{3}]] has two positive real solutions. What is their sum?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let [[t=\log_{2}x]], so [[x=2^{t}]]. The equation becomes [[2^{t^{2}}=2^{3t}]], hence [[t^{2}=3t]]. Thus [[t=0]] or [[t=3]], giving [[x=1]] or [[x=8]]. Their sum is 9.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Set [[t=\log_{2}x]] and rewrite [[x^{3}]] as [[(2^{t})^{3}=2^{3t}]]. Then equate the exponents.

screens.title: A derivative measures instantaneous change

diagram.caption: At P, the derivative is the gradient of the tangent to the curve.

screens.body:
For [[y=f(x)]], the derivative [[f'(x)]] gives the gradient of the tangent to the graph at each x-value.

It can also represent an instantaneous rate of change. For example, the derivative of displacement with respect to time is velocity.
screens.keyPoint: Differentiation from first principles is excluded from TMUA. You need to interpret derivatives and use the differentiation rules accurately.

screens.title: A tangent captures change at one instant

diagram.caption: The tangent gradient describes the behaviour of the curve at a single point.

steps.title: Average rate of change
steps.body: Between [[x=a]] and [[x=b]], the average rate of change is [[\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}]].

steps.title: Interpret the average
steps.body: This is the gradient of the straight line joining the two points on the curve.

steps.title: Instantaneous rate of change
steps.body: At the single point [[x=a]], the instantaneous rate of change is [[f'(a)]].

steps.title: Interpret the derivative
steps.body: [[f'(a)]] is the gradient of the tangent at [[(a,f(a))]]. It describes how quickly f is changing at that exact x-value.

screens.title: Recognise the different forms of notation
screens.body:
The first derivative may be written as [[f'(x)]] or [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}]]. These mean the same thing when [[y=f(x)]].

The second derivative may be written as [[f''(x)]] or [[\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}y}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}}]]. It is found by differentiating the first derivative.
screens.keyPoint: [[f'(x)]] is a function of x, while [[f'(a)]] is the numerical gradient at the particular point [[x=a]].

screens.title: Multiply by the power, then reduce it by 1

steps.title: Differentiate constants
steps.body: The derivative of a constant is zero because a constant does not change as x changes.

screens.question: Differentiate [[5x^{4}-3x^{2}+7x-9]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Differentiate each term to obtain [[f'(x)=20x^{3}-6x+7]]. The constant -9 differentiates to zero.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply each term by its old power, then reduce that power by 1. The derivative of -9 is zero.

screens.title: Rewrite roots and reciprocals before differentiating
screens.body:
The power rule also works with fractional and negative powers.

Rewrite roots using [[\sqrt{x}=x^{1/2}]] and reciprocals using [[\frac{1}{x^{k}}=x^{-k}]].

For example, [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(6x^{1/2})=3x^{-1/2}]] and [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(2x^{-3})=-6x^{-4}]].
screens.keyPoint: Simplifying the expression first often turns an unfamiliar quotient or root into an ordinary sum of powers.

screens.title: Differentiate an expression containing a root
screens.question: Given [[f(x)=\frac{3x^{2}-2x+5}{\sqrt{x}}]], find [[f'(x)]].

steps.title: Rewrite the denominator
steps.body: Since [[\sqrt{x}=x^{1/2}]], divide every term in the numerator by [[x^{1/2}]].

steps.title: Check the final term
steps.body: The derivative of [[5x^{-1/2}]] is [[-\frac52x^{-3/2}]], so its sign must be negative.

screens.finalAnswer: The derivative is [[\frac92x^{1/2}-x^{-1/2}-\frac52x^{-3/2}]], so the correct option is A.

screens.title: You can now differentiate rational powers.
screens.body:
You have differentiated sums and differences containing positive, fractional and negative powers.

Next, you will use derivatives to model rates of change and construct tangents and normals.

screens.question:
Differentiate
[[\frac{2x^{3}-7x^{2}}{3x\sqrt{x}}]].
screens.prompt: Cancel powers of x before applying the power rule.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. First simplify:

[[\frac{2x^{3}-7x^{2}}{3x\sqrt{x}}=\frac23x^{3/2}-\frac73x^{1/2}]].

Differentiating gives
[[x^{1/2}-\frac76x^{-1/2}=\frac{6x-7}{6\sqrt{x}}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Since [[3x\sqrt{x}=3x^{3/2}]], simplify the expression to [[\frac23x^{3/2}-\frac73x^{1/2}]] before differentiating.

screens.title: Differentiate again to measure how a rate changes
screens.body:
The second derivative is the derivative of the first derivative:

[[f''(x)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(f'(x))]].

If displacement is [[s(t)]], then velocity is [[v(t)=s'(t)]] and acceleration is [[a(t)=v'(t)=s''(t)]].
screens.keyPoint: Always check what the variables represent. A first derivative may be a velocity, while a second derivative may be an acceleration.

screens.question: A particle has displacement [[s(t)=t^{4}-3t^{3}+2t]]. What is its acceleration when [[t=2]]?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Acceleration is the second derivative of displacement. Differentiate s twice before substituting [[t=2]].

screens.title: Use the derivative to construct a line

diagram.caption: The tangent and normal pass through the same point on the curve.

screens.body:
At [[x=a]], the point on the curve is [[(a,f(a))]] and the tangent gradient is [[f'(a)]].

If this gradient is the non-zero value m, the normal gradient is [[-\frac1m]]. Use [[y-y_{1}=m(x-x_{1})]] to form either line.

If the tangent is horizontal, the normal is the vertical line [[x=a]].
screens.keyPoint: An equation of a tangent or normal requires both the correct gradient and the correct point.

screens.title: Simplify before finding the normal
screens.question: The curve [[y=\frac{x^{3}+2x}{\sqrt{x}}]] has a normal at [[x=1]]. Which equation is the normal?

steps.title: Find the point
steps.body: At [[x=1]], [[y=\frac{1+2}{1}=3]], so the line passes through [[(1,3)]].

steps.title: Find the tangent gradient
steps.body: [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=\frac52x^{3/2}+x^{-1/2}]], so at [[x=1]] the tangent gradient is [[\frac72]].

steps.title: Find the normal
