steps.body: The normal gradient is the negative reciprocal, [[-\frac27]]. Therefore its equation is [[y-3=-\frac27(x-1)]].

screens.finalAnswer: The normal is [[y-3=-\frac27(x-1)]], so the correct option is D.

screens.title: Differentiation: Gradients, Rates and Rules
keyPoints: [[f'(a)]] is the gradient of the tangent and the instantaneous rate of change at [[x=a]].
Use [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(x^{n})=nx^{n-1}]] for rational powers.
Rewrite roots, reciprocals and quotients as powers before differentiating.
The second derivative describes how the first derivative changes.
A normal has gradient [[-\frac1m]] when the tangent gradient is the non-zero value m.
reviewItems: Distinguish an average rate of change from an instantaneous rate of change.
Recognise [[f'(x)]], [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}]], [[f''(x)]] and [[\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}y}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}}]].
Check signs carefully when differentiating negative powers.
Use both the original function and its derivative when finding a tangent or normal.
Next lesson: Stationary Points and Curve Behaviour.

screens.title: A stationary point has zero gradient

diagram.caption: At a stationary point, the tangent to the curve is horizontal.

screens.body:
At a stationary point, the tangent is horizontal. Its gradient is therefore zero, so stationary points occur where

[[f'(x)=0]].

Solving this equation gives the possible x-coordinates. Substitute each value into the original function to find the complete coordinates.
screens.keyPoint: Solving [[f'(x)=0]] finds stationary x-values, not complete stationary points.

screens.title: Find the stationary point of a quadratic
screens.question: Find the stationary point of [[f(x)=x^{2}-6x+11]].

steps.title: Set the derivative equal to zero
Classify the point
steps.body: [[f''(x)=2>0]], so the point is a local minimum.

screens.finalAnswer: The stationary point is a local minimum at [[(3,2)]], so the correct option is B.

screens.question: At which x-values does [[f(x)=x^{3}-3x^{2}+2]] have stationary points?
screens.prompt: Solve [[f'(x)=0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f'(x)=3x^{2}-6x=3x(x-2)]], so the derivative is zero at [[x=0]] and [[x=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Differentiate first, then factor [[3x^{2}-6x]]. The stationary values are the roots of the derivative.

screens.title: Use [[f''(x)]] to classify stationary points
screens.body:
The second derivative describes how the gradient is changing.

At a stationary point [[x=a]]:

• If [[f''(a)>0]], the gradient is increasing through zero and the point is a local minimum.

• If [[f''(a)<0]], the gradient is decreasing through zero and the point is a local maximum.
screens.keyPoint: Find stationary points using [[f'(x)=0]] before applying the second derivative test.

screens.title: Classify both stationary points

diagram.caption: The actual graph has a local maximum at (-1, 10) and a local minimum at (3, -22).

steps.title: Find the coordinates
steps.body: [[f(-1)=10]] and [[f(3)=-22]], so the stationary points are [[(-1,10)]] and [[(3,-22)]].

steps.title: Classify both points
steps.body: [[f''(-1)=-12<0]], so [[(-1,10)]] is a local maximum. Also, [[f''(3)=12>0]], so [[(3,-22)]] is a local minimum.

screens.question:
The function f is defined by

[[f(x)=\left(\frac{3}{x}-\frac{1}{2x^{2}}\right)^{2}]], [[x\ne0]].

Find [[f''(1)]].
screens.prompt: Expand the square and rewrite using negative powers before differentiating twice.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Expand the square first:

[[f(x)=9x^{-2}-3x^{-3}+\frac14x^{-4}]].

Apply the power rule twice, then substitute [[x=1]].

screens.title: You can now find and classify turning points.
screens.body:
You have used the first derivative to locate stationary points and the second derivative to classify maxima and minima.

Next, we will use the sign of the derivative to describe the behaviour of a function across whole intervals.

screens.title: The sign of the derivative controls direction
screens.body:
A function is strictly increasing wherever [[f'(x)>0]] and strictly decreasing wherever [[f'(x)<0]].

The roots of [[f'(x)]] divide the number line into intervals. Test the sign of the derivative in each interval to determine whether the original function rises or falls.
screens.keyPoint: Use the sign of [[f'(x)]], not the sign of [[f(x)]]. A negative function can still be increasing.

screens.title: Find intervals of increase and decrease
screens.question: For [[f(x)=x^{3}-12x]], where is f strictly increasing?

steps.title: Find the critical values
steps.body: [[f'(x)=0]] at [[x=-2]] and [[x=2]]. These values split the number line into three intervals.

steps.title: Check the sign
steps.body: The derivative is positive for [[x<-2]], negative for [[-2<x<2]], and positive for [[x>2]].

steps.title: State the answer
steps.body: Therefore f is strictly increasing when [[x<-2]] or [[x>2]].

screens.finalAnswer: The correct option is D.

screens.question: For which values of x is [[f(x)=x^{3}+3x^{2}-9x]] strictly decreasing?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f'(x)=3x^{2}+6x-9=3(x+3)(x-1)]]. This is negative between its roots, so f decreases for [[-3<x<1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor the derivative and determine where the product [[3(x+3)(x-1)]] is negative.

screens.eyebrow: WHEN THE TEST IS INCONCLUSIVE · 11 OF 14
screens.title: [[f''(a)=0]] does not classify the point
screens.body:
If [[f''(a)=0]], the second derivative test is inconclusive. The point might be a maximum, a minimum or neither.

Instead, examine the sign of [[f'(x)]] on either side of a:

• Positive to negative means a local maximum.

• Negative to positive means a local minimum.

• No sign change means it is not a turning point.

A point of inflexion is where the direction of curvature changes. TMUA does not require formal calculations involving points of inflexion, but you should recognise their general shape.
screens.keyPoint: A zero second derivative is not itself evidence of a point of inflexion.

screens.title: Classify the stationary point of [[f(x)=x^{4}]]

steps.title: Try the second derivative
steps.body: [[f''(x)=12x^{2}]], so [[f''(0)=0]]. The second derivative test is inconclusive.

steps.title: Check the derivative to the left
steps.body: When [[x<0]], [[4x^{3}<0]], so the function is decreasing.

steps.title: Check the derivative to the right
steps.body: When [[x>0]], [[4x^{3}>0]], so the function is increasing. Therefore [[(0,0)]] is a local minimum.

screens.question:
The curve has equation

[[y=(5p-x^{2})(px+4)]].

The gradient at [[x=2]] depends on p. For which value of p is this gradient least?
screens.prompt: Expand before differentiating, then minimise the resulting quadratic in p.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. First expand:

[[y=5p^{2}x+20p-px^{3}-4x^{2}]].

Differentiate with respect to x:

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=5p^{2}-3px^{2}-8x]].

At [[x=2]], the gradient is
[[5p^{2}-12p-16]].

This upward-opening quadratic is minimised when
[[p=-\frac{-12}{2(5)}=\frac65]].
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Expand the brackets before differentiating. At [[x=2]], the gradient becomes

[[5p^{2}-12p-16]].

Use [[p=-\frac{b}{2a}]] to find where this quadratic is smallest.

screens.title: Stationary Points and Curve Behaviour: key points
keyPoints: Stationary points occur where [[f'(x)=0]].
Substitute stationary x-values into f to find complete coordinates.
At a stationary point, [[f''(x)>0]] indicates a local minimum and [[f''(x)<0]] indicates a local maximum.
A function increases where [[f'(x)>0]] and decreases where [[f'(x)<0]].
If the second derivative test is inconclusive, examine how the sign of [[f'(x)]] changes.
reviewItems: Factor derivatives carefully before solving.
Do not forget the y-coordinate of a stationary point.
Use the original function to find coordinates and the derivatives to study behaviour.
Build sign charts from the derivative rather than the original function.
Next lesson: Integration and Area.

screens.title: Integration reverses differentiation
screens.body:
If [[F'(x)=f(x)]], then

[[\int f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=F(x)+C]].

The function F is called an antiderivative of f. The constant C is required because every constant disappears when differentiated.
screens.keyPoint: Differentiate an indefinite integral mentally to check it. Without [[+C]], the answer represents only one member of the family of antiderivatives.

screens.title: Increase the power, then divide

steps.title: Integrate a negative power
steps.body: [[\int-4x^{-2}\,\mathrm{d}x=4x^{-1}+C]]. Dividing by a negative new power may change the sign.

screens.question:
Find
[[\int(6x^{2}-4x+5)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Integrating term by term gives [[2x^{3}-2x^{2}+5x+C]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Increase each power by 1 and divide by the new power. Also remember that [[\int5\,\mathrm{d}x=5x]].

screens.title: Rewrite roots and reciprocals first
screens.body:
The reverse power rule works with fractional and negative powers.

Use [[\sqrt{x}=x^{1/2}]] and [[\frac1{x^{k}}=x^{-k}]]. If an entire expression is divided by a power of x, divide every term before integrating.
screens.keyPoint: The case [[x^{-1}]] is excluded from this power rule because increasing its power would produce zero in the denominator.

screens.title: Divide through by the root
screens.question:
Find
[[\int\frac{6x^{2}-4x}{\sqrt{x}}\,\mathrm{d}x]].

steps.title: Integrate the second term
steps.body: [[\int-4x^{1/2}\,\mathrm{d}x=-\frac83x^{3/2}]]. Therefore the correct option is A.

screens.finalAnswer: The integral is [[\frac{12}{5}x^{5/2}-\frac83x^{3/2}+C]].

screens.question:
Find
[[\int\frac{4x^{3}-3x}{2x\sqrt{x}}\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.prompt: Rewrite the denominator as a power of x.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The integrand simplifies to [[2x^{3/2}-\frac32x^{-1/2}]]. Integrating gives [[\frac45x^{5/2}-3x^{1/2}+C]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Since [[2x\sqrt{x}=2x^{3/2}]], simplify the integrand to [[2x^{3/2}-\frac32x^{-1/2}]] before integrating.

screens.title: You can now recover a family of functions.
screens.body:
You have integrated positive, fractional and negative powers and simplified expressions before applying the reverse power rule.

Next, limits will select a numerical amount of accumulated change.

screens.title: Definite integrals measure accumulated change
screens.body:
If [[F'(x)=f(x)]], then

[[\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=F(b)-F(a)]].

A definite integral is a number, so it does not require [[+C]]. The theorem also gives

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}\left(\int_{a}^{x}f(t)\,\mathrm{d}t\right)=f(x)]].
screens.keyPoint: Use upper limit minus lower limit: [[\left[F(x)\right]_{a}^{b}=F(b)-F(a)]].

screens.title: Integrate before applying the limits
screens.question:
Evaluate
[[\int_{1}^{4}(3x^{1/2}-2x^{-1/2})\,\mathrm{d}x]].

steps.title: Subtract the lower value
steps.body: At [[x=1]], the value is [[2-4=-2]]. Therefore the integral is [[8-(-2)=10]].

screens.finalAnswer: The value of the definite integral is 10, so the correct option is C.

screens.question:
Define [[F(x)]] by

[[F(x)=]]
[[\int_{0}^{x}(3t^{2}-4t+k)\,\mathrm{d}t]].

It is known that [[F(2)=F(1)]].

Find [[F(3)]].
screens.prompt: Use the equality to determine k before calculating F(3).
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[F(2)=F(1)]],

[[F(2)-F(1)=\int_{1}^{2}(3t^{2}-4t+k)\,\mathrm{d}t=0]].

Using the antiderivative [[t^{3}-2t^{2}+kt]] gives
[[k+1=0]], so [[k=-1]].

Therefore
[[F(3)=\int_{0}^{3}(3t^{2}-4t-1)\,\mathrm{d}t=6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Begin with

[[F(2)-F(1)=\int_{1}^{2}(3t^{2}-4t+k)\,\mathrm{d}t]].

The equality [[F(2)=F(1)]] means this integral is zero. Use it to find k, then evaluate F(3).

screens.title: An integral is not always geometric area

diagram.caption: Regions above the x-axis contribute positively and regions below contribute negatively.

screens.body:
The definite integral [[\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]] gives signed area.

Area above the x-axis counts positively, while area below it counts negatively. Geometric area is always non-negative, so a curve crossing the axis requires the interval to be split at each root.
screens.keyPoint: Integral and geometric area are equal only when the function does not go below the x-axis on the interval.

screens.title: Split the interval at the root
screens.question: Find the total area between [[y=x^{2}-4]] and the x-axis for [[0\le x\le3]].

steps.title: Find the crossing
steps.body: [[x^{2}-4=0]] gives [[x=2]] in the interval. The curve is below the axis from 0 to 2 and above it from 2 to 3.

screens.finalAnswer: The total area is [[\frac{23}{3}]], so the correct option is D.

screens.title: Integral intervals behave like directed journeys
screens.body:
Adjacent intervals can be joined:

[[\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x+\int_{b}^{c}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]]
[[=\int_{a}^{c}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].

Reversing the direction changes the sign:

[[\int_{b}^{a}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]]
[[=-\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].

Over equal limits, sums can be integrated term by term.
screens.keyPoint:
For constants r and s:

[[\int_{a}^{b}(rf(x)+sg(x))\,\mathrm{d}x]]
[[=r\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x+s\int_{a}^{b}g(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].

screens.question:
For every integer [[n\ge0]],

[[\int_{n}^{n+1}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=2n+1]].

Evaluate [[\int_{0}^{4}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Split the interval into four adjacent parts:

[[\int_{0}^{4}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=1+3+5+7=16]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[n=0,1,2,3]] for the four intervals from 0 to 4. Their values are 1, 3, 5 and 7.

screens.title: Integration, Definite Integrals and Area
keyPoints: For [[n\ne-1]], [[\int x^{n}\,\mathrm{d}x=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C]].
Rewrite roots, reciprocals and quotients using powers before integrating.
Definite integrals use [[F(b)-F(a)]] and do not require an arbitrary constant.
A definite integral gives signed accumulation, which may differ from geometric area.
Contiguous intervals join, while reversing the limits reverses the sign.
reviewItems: Differentiate indefinite answers to check them.
Use clear brackets when substituting limits.
Split total-area calculations wherever the curve crosses the x-axis.
Use integral identities before attempting unnecessary calculations.
Next lesson: Trapezium Rule, Differential Equations and Total Change.

screens.title: Approximate a curved region using trapezia

diagram.caption: Straight chords replace short sections of the curve.

screens.body:
Suppose an interval is divided into n equal strips of width h, with ordinates

[[y_{0},y_{1},\ldots,y_{n}]].

The trapezium-rule estimate is

[[\frac{h}{2}\left(y_{0}+2(y_{1}+\cdots+y_{n-1})+y_{n}\right)]].
screens.keyPoint: The two endpoint ordinates appear once. Every ordinate inside the interval appears twice.

screens.title: Find the strip width and count the ordinates

steps.title: Find the width
steps.body:
For n equal strips between a and b,

[[h=\frac{b-a}{n}]].

steps.title: Count the values
steps.body: There are always [[n+1]] ordinates because neighbouring strips share their boundary values.

steps.title: Identify the interior values
steps.body: [[y_{1},\ldots,y_{n-1}]] are each counted twice because they belong to two adjacent trapezia.

screens.question:
At

[[x=0,\frac32,3,\frac92]],

the ordinates of a curve are respectively

[[2,5,4,8]].

Use the trapezium rule to estimate the integral.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. There are three strips of width [[h=\frac32]]. Therefore

[[T=\frac34\left(2+2(5+4)+8\right)]]
[[=\frac34(28)=21]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: There are three strips, not four. Their width is [[\frac32]]. Count the two interior ordinates twice.

screens.title: Curvature determines the direction of the error

diagram.caption: The rule uses straight chords in place of the curved graph.

screens.body:
If the graph is concave upwards, its chords lie above the curve, so the trapezium rule gives an overestimate.

If the graph is concave downwards, its chords lie below the curve, so the rule gives an underestimate.
screens.keyPoint: Where [[f''(x)>0]], expect an overestimate. Where [[f''(x)<0]], expect an underestimate.

screens.question:
The trapezium rule is used to estimate an integral of

[[f(x)=6-2x^{2}]].

Which statement is correct?
options: It is an overestimate because f may be positive.
It is an overestimate because [[f'(x)<0]] for positive x.
It is exact because f is a polynomial.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[f''(x)=-4<0]], the graph is concave downwards and each chord lies below the curve.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Whether the function is positive or increasing is not decisive. Use the sign of the second derivative.

screens.title: Compare a trapezium estimate with a geometric integral
screens.question:
The trapezium rule with three equal strips estimates

[[\int_{0}^{3}\sqrt{9-x^{2}}\,\mathrm{d}x]].

By how much does the exact value exceed the estimate?

steps.title: Find the ordinates
steps.body:
The strip width is 1. At [[x=0,1,2,3]], the ordinates are

[[3,2\sqrt2,\sqrt5,0]].

steps.title: Find the exact integral
steps.body:
The curve is the upper quarter of the circle [[x^{2}+y^{2}=9]]. Its exact area is

[[\frac14\pi(3^{2})=\frac{9\pi}{4}]].

steps.title: Find the positive difference
steps.body:
The graph is concave down, so the estimate is smaller. The required difference is

[[\frac{9\pi}{4}-\frac32-2\sqrt2-\sqrt5]].

screens.finalAnswer: The correct option is A.

screens.question:
Use the trapezium rule with three equal strips to estimate

[[\int_{1}^{4}\log_{2}x\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.prompt: Leave the answer as a single logarithm.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Here [[h=1]], and the ordinates are

[[0,1,\log_{2}3,2]].

Therefore
[[T=\frac12\left(0+2(1+\log_{2}3)+2\right)]]
[[=2+\log_{2}3=\log_{2}12]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the values at [[x=1,2,3,4]]. After applying the trapezium rule, combine [[2+\log_{2}3]] into one logarithm.

screens.title: You can calculate and interpret trapezium estimates.
screens.body:
You have handled non-unit spacing, exact comparisons, logarithmic ordinates and the direction of the error.

Next, integration will be used to recover a function from its derivative.

screens.title: Recover y from its derivative
screens.body:
For the differential equations required by TMUA, the derivative has the form

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=f(x)]].

Integrating both sides gives

[[y=\int f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=F(x)+C]].

A point on the curve or an initial condition can then be used to determine C.
screens.keyPoint: The general solution contains C. A given condition selects one particular curve from that family.

screens.title: Find a particular solution
screens.question:
Given

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=6x^{2}-4x+1]]

and [[y=2]] when [[x=1]], find y in terms of x.
screens.finalAnswer: The correct option is A.

screens.question:
A differential equation is given by

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=|8x|]]

for all real x. Which is a possible general solution?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. For [[x\ge0]], [[4x|x|=4x^{2}]], whose derivative is [[8x]]. For [[x<0]], [[4x|x|=-4x^{2}]], whose derivative is [[-8x]]. These are both equal to [[|8x|]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Consider positive and negative x separately. The expression must differentiate to [[8x]] when x is positive and to [[-8x]] when x is negative.

screens.title: Integrating a rate gives the net change
screens.body:
If a quantity Q changes at rate [[Q'(t)]], then

[[Q(b)-Q(a)=\int_{a}^{b}Q'(t)\,\mathrm{d}t]].

Therefore:

final value = initial value
+ accumulated change.
screens.keyPoint: A negative integral represents a decrease in the quantity, not a negative geometric area.

screens.title: Find the least value of a changing quantity
screens.question:
For [[t\ge1]], a quantity Q satisfies

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}Q}{\mathrm{d}t}=3(t-1)(t-3)]].

Given [[Q(1)=5]], what is the least possible value of Q?

steps.title: Find when Q decreases
steps.body: For [[1<t<3]], the rate [[3(t-1)(t-3)]] is negative. For [[t>3]], it is positive.

steps.title: Locate the minimum
steps.body: Therefore Q decreases until [[t=3]] and increases afterwards. Its least value occurs at [[t=3]].

steps.title: Evaluate
steps.body:
An antiderivative is [[t^{3}-6t^{2}+9t]]. The integral from 1 to 3 is [[0-4=-4]]. Hence

[[Q(3)=5-4=1]].

screens.finalAnswer: The least possible value is 1, so the correct option is B.

screens.title: Trapezium Rule, Differential Equations and Change
keyPoints: For n equal strips, [[h=\frac{b-a}{n}]] and there are [[n+1]] ordinates.
Count the two endpoint ordinates once and every interior ordinate twice.
Concave-up curves give overestimates; concave-down curves give underestimates.
Integrate [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=f(x)]] and use any given condition to determine C.
The integral of a rate over an interval gives the net change in the quantity.
reviewItems: Never assume that the strip width is 1.
Use geometry when an exact integral represents a familiar shape.
Keep the arbitrary constant until a condition determines it.
Distinguish accumulated change from geometric area.
Next lesson: Calculus Exam-style Questions.

screens.question:
Differentiate

[[\frac{3x^{4}-5x^{2}}{2x\sqrt{x}}]].
screens.prompt: Simplify the powers of x before differentiating.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. First simplify:

[[\frac{3x^{4}-5x^{2}}{2x\sqrt{x}}]]
[[=\frac32x^{5/2}-\frac52x^{1/2}]].

Differentiating gives
[[\frac{15}{4}x^{3/2}-\frac54x^{-1/2}]]
[[=\frac{5(3x^{2}-1)}{4\sqrt{x}}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite the denominator as [[2x^{3/2}]], simplify both terms, and then apply the power rule.

screens.question:
The function

[[f(x)=x^{4}+ax^{3}+bx^{2}]]

has a stationary point at [[x=1]]. It is also given that [[f''(1)=0]]. Find [[a+b]].
screens.prompt: Use the two derivative conditions to form simultaneous equations.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since [[x=1]] is stationary,

[[f'(1)=4+3a+2b=0]].

Also,
[[f''(1)=12+6a+2b=0]].

Subtracting the first equation from the second gives [[8+3a=0]], so [[a=-\frac83]]. Substitution gives [[b=2]]. Therefore

[[a+b=-\frac83+2=-\frac23]].
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Differentiate twice:

[[f'(x)=4x^{3}+3ax^{2}+2bx]]

and
[[f''(x)=12x^{2}+6ax+2b]].

Use [[f'(1)=0]] and [[f''(1)=0]] as simultaneous equations.

screens.question:
The curve has equation

[[y=\frac{x^{2}+3}{\sqrt{x}}]].

Find the normal at [[x=4]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Rewrite
[[y=x^{3/2}+3x^{-1/2}]].

At [[x=4]], the point is [[\left(4,\frac{19}{2}\right)]], and the tangent gradient is [[\frac{45}{16}]]. The normal gradient is therefore [[-\frac{16}{45}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the point using the original function. Then differentiate, substitute [[x=4]], and take the negative reciprocal of the tangent gradient.

screens.question:
The curve has equation

[[y=x^{4}+qx^{3}+(2q^{2}-5q)x^{2}]].

As q varies, what is the least possible gradient of the curve at [[x=1]]?
screens.prompt: Express the gradient at x = 1 as a quadratic in q.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Differentiating gives

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=4x^{3}+3qx^{2}+2(2q^{2}-5q)x]].

At [[x=1]], the gradient is

[[4q^{2}-7q+4]].

Completing the square gives

[[4\left(q-\frac78\right)^{2}+\frac{15}{16}]].

Therefore the least possible gradient is [[\frac{15}{16}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Differentiate with respect to x and then substitute [[x=1]]. Complete the square in the resulting expression [[4q^{2}-7q+4]]. Be careful: the question asks for the minimum gradient, not the value of q where it occurs.

screens.question:
The function

[[f(x)=x^{3}+px^{2}+qx+7]]

has stationary points when [[x=-1]] and [[x=3]]. Find [[p-q]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since
[[f'(x)=3x^{2}+2px+q]],

its roots are -1 and 3. Therefore
[[f'(x)=3(x+1)(x-3)]]
[[=3x^{2}-6x-9]].

Hence [[2p=-6]] and [[q=-9]], giving [[p-q=6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Form the quadratic derivative whose roots are -1 and 3, then compare its coefficients with [[3x^{2}+2px+q]].

screens.question:
The function is defined for [[x>0]] by

[[f(x)=\frac{x^{2}+8}{\sqrt{x}}]].

Where is f strictly decreasing?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Differentiating gives

[[f'(x)=\frac{3x^{2}-8}{2x^{3/2}}]].

The denominator is positive for [[x>0]], so [[f'(x)<0]] precisely when [[3x^{2}-8<0]], giving [[0
screens.incorrectFeedback: For [[x>0]], the denominator of the derivative is positive. Determine where its numerator is negative.

screens.question:
Let

[[f(x)=x^{3}-3x^{2}-9x]]

and

[[g(x)=x^{3}-3x^{2}-24x]].

For which values of x is f increasing while g is decreasing?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. We have

[[f'(x)=3(x-3)(x+1)]],

so f increases for [[x<-1]] or [[x>3]]. Also,

[[g'(x)=3(x-4)(x+2)]],

so g decreases for [[-2
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find where [[f'(x)>0]] and where [[g'(x)<0]], then take the intersection of the two solution sets.

screens.question:
A differentiable function satisfies

[[f'(x)=(x+2)^{2}(x-1)]].

Which statement must be true?
options: f has a local maximum at [[x=-2]] and a local minimum at [[x=1]].
f is stationary at [[x=-2]], but has no local extremum there; it has a local minimum at [[x=1]].
f has a local minimum at [[x=-2]] and a local maximum at [[x=1]].
f is strictly increasing except at its two stationary points.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The squared factor does not change sign at [[x=-2]], so the derivative remains negative on both sides and there is no extremum. At [[x=1]], the derivative changes from negative to positive, giving a local minimum.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Make a sign chart for the derivative. A repeated root of even multiplicity does not cause its sign to change.

screens.question:
For which values of k is

[[f'(x)>0]]

for every real x, where

[[f(x)=x^{3}+3kx^{2}+3x]]?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. We have

[[f'(x)=3(x^{2}+2kx+1)]].

The quadratic must be strictly positive for every real x. Since it opens upwards, this requires its discriminant to be negative:
[[4k^{2}-4<0]],

so [[-1
screens.incorrectFeedback: Require the quadratic inside the derivative to have no real roots. Equality would make the derivative zero at one point, so the inequalities must be strict.

screens.question:
Which statement correctly describes the stationary points of

[[f(x)=x^{4}-8x^{2}+3]]?
options: There is one local minimum at [[(0,3)]].
There are local maxima at [[(-2,-13)]] and [[(2,-13)]].
All three stationary points are local minima.
There are local minima at [[(-2,-13)]] and [[(2,-13)]], and a local maximum at [[(0,3)]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since
[[f'(x)=4x(x-2)(x+2)]],

the stationary values are [[x=-2,0,2]]. Also,
[[f''(x)=12x^{2}-16]].

This is positive at [[x=\pm2]] and negative at [[x=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Solve [[f'(x)=0]], calculate all three coordinates, and use the sign of the second derivative at each point.

screens.question:
Find

[[\int\frac{6x^{3}-5x}{2x\sqrt{x}}\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The integrand simplifies to

[[3x^{3/2}-\frac52x^{-1/2}]].

Integrating gives
[[\frac65x^{5/2}-5x^{1/2}+C]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite [[2x\sqrt{x}]] as [[2x^{3/2}]] and simplify both numerator terms before integrating.

screens.question:
Evaluate

[[\int_{1}^{9}\left(2\sqrt{x}-\frac3{\sqrt{x}}\right)\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. An antiderivative is

[[\frac43x^{3/2}-6x^{1/2}]].

Its value at 9 is 18, and its value at 1 is [[-\frac{14}{3}]]. Therefore the integral is [[18+\frac{14}{3}=\frac{68}{3}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite the root and reciprocal root as fractional powers, integrate, and use upper limit minus lower limit.

screens.question:
Define

[[H(x)=\int_{0}^{x}(3t^{2}+kt-4)\,\mathrm{d}t]].

The graph of H has a stationary point when [[x=2]]. Find [[H(2)]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. By the Fundamental Theorem,

[[H'(x)=3x^{2}+kx-4]].

The stationary-point condition gives [[H'(2)=0]], so [[8+2k=0]] and [[k=-4]]. Therefore

[[H(2)=\int_{0}^{2}(3t^{2}-4t-4)\,\mathrm{d}t=-8]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the Fundamental Theorem to find [[H'(x)]]. The stationary condition determines k before the definite integral is evaluated.

screens.question:
Find the total area between

[[y=x^{2}-5x+4]]

and the x-axis for [[0\le x\le5]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The curve crosses the axis at [[x=1]] and [[x=4]]. It is positive on [[0,1]], negative on [[1,4]], and positive on [[4,5]]. Evaluating the three positive regions gives a total area of [[\frac{49}{6}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor the quadratic to find both roots. Split the interval at those roots and make the below-axis integral positive.

screens.question:
A function f satisfies

[[\int_{0}^{6}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=2]]

and

[[\int_{0}^{6}|f(x)|\,\mathrm{d}x=14]].

It is known that f is non-negative on [[0\le x\le2]] and non-positive on [[2\le x\le6]]. Find

[[\int_{6}^{2}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Let the positive area be P and the magnitude of the negative area be N. Then

[[P-N=2]],
[[P+N=14]].

Hence [[P=8]] and [[N=6]]. Therefore [[\int_{2}^{6}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=-6]], so reversing the limits gives 6.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the signed integral and the absolute-value integral to form two simultaneous equations for the positive and negative areas.

screens.question:
For every integer [[n\ge0]],

[[\int_{n}^{n+1}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=3n-1]].

Evaluate
[[\int_{5}^{1}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. From 1 to 5, use [[n=1,2,3,4]]:

[[2+5+8+11=26]].

The required limits are reversed, so the value is -26.
screens.incorrectFeedback: First add the four contiguous integrals from 1 to 5. Then account for the reversed limits.

screens.question:
Evaluate

[[\sum_{r=1}^{4}\int_{r}^{r+1}(2x-r)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. For a fixed r,

[[\int_{r}^{r+1}(2x-r)\,\mathrm{d}x=r+1]].

Therefore the required sum is
[[2+3+4+5=14]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Evaluate the integral in terms of r first. Only then substitute [[r=1,2,3,4]] and add the results.

screens.question:
At

[[x=0,\frac12,1,\frac32,2]],

the ordinates of a curve are respectively

[[4,5,8,13,20]].

The curve satisfies [[f''(x)>0]] throughout the interval. Which statement is correct?
options: The trapezium estimate is 19 and is an underestimate.
The trapezium estimate is 19 and is an overestimate.
The trapezium estimate is 38 and is an overestimate.
The trapezium estimate is 38 and is an underestimate.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Here [[h=\frac12]], so

[[T=\frac14(4+2(5+8+13)+20)=19]].

Since [[f''(x)>0]], the graph is concave upwards and the trapezium rule gives an overestimate.
screens.incorrectFeedback: There are four strips of width [[\frac12]]. Count the internal ordinates twice, then use the positive second derivative to determine the error direction.

screens.question:
A function satisfies

[[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=6x^{2}-4x^{-1/2}]]

and [[y=3]] when [[x=1]]. Find y when [[x=4]].
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Integrating gives

[[y=2x^{3}-8x^{1/2}+C]].

The initial condition gives [[C=9]]. Therefore
[[y(4)=128-16+9=121]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Integrate both terms and include an arbitrary constant. Use the value at [[x=1]] before substituting [[x=4]].

screens.question:
A rectangle is symmetric about the y-axis. Its upper vertices lie on

[[y=12-x^{2}]],

and its lower vertices lie on

[[y=x^{2}]].

What is the greatest possible area of the rectangle?
screens.prompt: Let the vertical sides have equations x = u and x = -u.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. At [[x=\pm u]], the width is [[2u]] and the height is

[[12-u^{2}-u^{2}=12-2u^{2}]].

Hence
[[A(u)=24u-4u^{3}]].

Solving [[A'(u)=24-12u^{2}=0]] gives [[u=\sqrt2]]. Therefore the maximum area is [[16\sqrt2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Express the width and vertical distance between the curves in terms of u. Differentiate the resulting area function and find its positive stationary value.

screens.title: A useful sketch records structure, not perfect scale

diagram.caption: The overall shape, intercepts, symmetry and end behaviour matter more than exact proportions.

screens.body:
A mathematical sketch should show the features that determine the graph: intercepts, symmetry, turning points or sharp corners, asymptotes, endpoints, periodicity, and end behaviour.

Do not spend time making a sketch look artistic. Place the important features in the correct order and label any values that are known exactly.
screens.keyPoint: Before drawing, ask: where is the function defined, where can it cross an axis, and what happens at the edges of its domain?

screens.title: A line is determined by its gradient and intercept

diagram.caption: For y = mx + c, m controls the direction and steepness while c gives the y-intercept.

screens.body:
For [[y=mx+c]], a positive m gives an increasing line and a negative m gives a decreasing line. When [[m=0]], the graph is horizontal.

The graph crosses the y-axis at [[(0,c)]]. If [[m\ne0]], its x-intercept is found from [[mx+c=0]], giving [[x=-\frac{c}{m}]].
screens.keyPoint: A vertical line has equation [[x=k]] and cannot be written in the form [[y=mx+c]].

screens.question: Which description is correct for [[y=-3x+6]]?
options: It is increasing and crosses the x-axis at [[x=-2]].
It is decreasing and crosses the x-axis at [[x=2]].
It is decreasing and crosses the y-axis at [[y=-3]].
It is horizontal and crosses the y-axis at [[y=6]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The gradient is negative, so the line decreases. Setting [[y=0]] gives [[-3x+6=0]], hence [[x=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read the sign of the coefficient of x, then set [[y=0]] to locate the x-intercept.

screens.title: A quadratic has one turning point and a line of symmetry

diagram.caption: The parent graph y = x² is symmetric about the y-axis and has a minimum at the origin.

screens.body:
A graph with a non-zero [[x^{2}]] term and no higher powers is a parabola. A positive leading coefficient makes it open upwards; a negative one makes it open downwards.

A quadratic may cross the x-axis twice, touch it once, or miss it completely. Its turning point is a minimum when it opens upwards and a maximum when it opens downwards.
screens.keyPoint: The axis of symmetry passes through the turning point and lies midway between two real roots.

screens.question: Which set of features belongs to [[y=(x-2)^{2}-9]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The completed-square form gives the minimum [[(2,-9)]]. Solving [[(x-2)^{2}=9]] gives [[x=-1]] or [[x=5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read the turning point from completed-square form, then solve for the x-intercepts.

screens.title: A positive cubic runs from bottom-left to top-right

diagram.caption: The parent cubic y = x³ has rotational symmetry about the origin and passes through it.

screens.body:
For the parent graph [[y=x^{3}]], the domain and range are both all real numbers. It is increasing throughout and has rotational symmetry about the origin.

A general cubic can bend to create a local maximum and minimum. With real coefficients it has at least one real root, and it may have three distinct real roots.
screens.keyPoint: The sign of the leading [[x^{3}]] coefficient determines the end behaviour: positive rises to the right; negative falls to the right.

screens.title: Build a cubic sketch from algebraic features
screens.question: Which description matches [[h(x)=x^{3}-4x]]?
options: It has roots [[-2,0,2]], rotational symmetry about the origin, and rises to the right.
It has roots [[-4,0,4]], symmetry about the y-axis, and rises to the right.
It has roots [[-2,2]], rotational symmetry about the origin, and falls to the right.
It has one root at [[0]] and no other x-intercepts.

steps.title: Check symmetry
steps.body: [[h(-x)=-x^{3}+4x=-h(x)]], so h is odd and has rotational symmetry about the origin.

steps.title: Read the end behaviour
steps.body: The leading term is [[+x^{3}]], so the graph goes down to the left and up to the right.

steps.title: Combine the evidence
steps.body: The curve must cross the x-axis at [[-2,0,2]] in that order while keeping the cubic end behaviour.

screens.finalAnswer: The correct description is A.

screens.title: You can now distinguish lines, quadratics and cubics from their structure.
screens.body:
You have used gradient, symmetry, roots, turning points and end behaviour rather than relying on a memorised picture.

Next come graphs with restricted domains, sharp corners and asymptotes.

screens.title: Endpoints and corners reveal the function family

diagram.caption: The square-root graph has an endpoint; the modulus graph has a sharp vertex.

screens.body:
The parent square-root graph [[y=\sqrt{x}]] starts at [[(0,0)]] and exists only for [[x\ge0]]. It increases but gradually becomes less steep.

The modulus graph [[y=\lvert x\rvert]] is made from two straight rays. It is defined for every real x, has range [[y\ge0]], and has a sharp corner at the origin.
screens.keyPoint: A smooth U-shaped minimum suggests a quadratic; a sharp V-shaped minimum suggests a modulus graph.

screens.question: Which statement distinguishes [[y=\sqrt{x}]] from [[y=\lvert x\rvert]]?
options: Only [[y=\sqrt{x}]] is defined for negative x.
Only [[y=\sqrt{x}]] has a restricted domain and an endpoint.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Both graphs pass through the origin and have non-negative range, but [[\sqrt{x}]] is defined only for [[x\ge0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Compare their domains as well as their ranges.

screens.title: A reciprocal graph approaches axes without meeting them

diagram.caption: For y = 1/x, x = 0 and y = 0 are asymptotes.

screens.body:
For [[y=\frac1x]], the function is undefined at [[x=0]], and its output is never zero. The coordinate axes are asymptotes.

The parent graph has two branches in the first and third quadrants and rotational symmetry about the origin. It decreases on each of the intervals [[x<0]] and [[x>0]].
screens.keyPoint: Crossing an asymptote is not automatically impossible for every function, but the parent reciprocal graph never crosses either axis.

screens.question: Which feature belongs to [[y=\frac1x]] but not to [[y=x^{3}]]?
options: A domain containing every real number.
A vertical asymptote and no x-intercept.
A branch in the first quadrant.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Both have origin symmetry and points in the first quadrant, but [[1/x]] is undefined at zero and never equals zero.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Look for the feature caused by division by x.

screens.title: Inverse functions swap domain, range and asymptotes

diagram.caption: For base 2, the exponential passes through (0,1) and the logarithm passes through (1,0).

screens.body:
For a base [[a>1]], [[y=a^{x}]] is positive for every real x, passes through [[(0,1)]], and has horizontal asymptote [[y=0]].

Its inverse [[y=\log_{a}x]] is defined only for [[x>0]], passes through [[(1,0)]], and has vertical asymptote [[x=0]]. Their graphs are reflections of each other in [[y=x]].
screens.keyPoint: An exponential has unrestricted x-values but positive outputs; a logarithm has positive x-values but unrestricted outputs.

screens.question: Which ordered pair correctly gives the domain and range of [[y=\log_{2}x]]?
options: Domain: all real x except 0; range: all real y except 0.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. A logarithm accepts only positive inputs, but its output can be any real number.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the fact that logarithms and exponentials exchange domain and range.

screens.title: Periodicity separates trigonometric graphs from the other families

diagram.caption: Sine and cosine repeat every 2π; tangent repeats every π and has vertical asymptotes.

screens.body:
The graphs of [[\sin x]] and [[\cos x]] have range [[-1\le y\le1]] and period [[2\pi]]. Sine passes through the origin, while cosine begins at 1 when [[x=0]].

The tangent graph has period [[\pi]], range all real numbers, zeros at integer multiples of [[\pi]], and vertical asymptotes halfway between them.
screens.keyPoint: At [[x=0]], the values [[\sin0=0]], [[\cos0=1]], and [[\tan0=0]] quickly distinguish the three parent graphs.

screens.question: A periodic graph has range all real numbers and is undefined at [[x=\frac{\pi}{2}]]. Which parent function matches it?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Tangent has vertical asymptotes at [[x=\frac{\pi}{2}+k\pi]] and takes every real y-value between them.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Sine and cosine are defined everywhere and remain between -1 and 1.

screens.question: A graph is continuous for all real x, has rotational symmetry about the origin, passes through [[(0,0)]], and has a horizontal tangent there. Which parent graph fits all four features?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[y=x^{3}]] is continuous and odd, passes through the origin, and flattens there. The line [[y=x]] does not have a horizontal tangent.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use every condition. In particular, compare the behaviour at the origin.

screens.title: Recognising and Sketching Common Graphs: key points
keyPoints: Use intercepts, symmetry, turning points, endpoints, corners, asymptotes and end behaviour to build a sketch.
Lines, quadratics and cubics are distinguished by gradient, turning-point structure and end behaviour.
Square-root graphs have restricted domains; modulus graphs have sharp vertices; reciprocal graphs have separate branches and asymptotes.
Exponential and logarithmic graphs are inverse shapes with exchanged domains, ranges and asymptotes.
Sine, cosine and tangent are recognised through starting values, ranges, periods and asymptotes.
reviewItems: Sketch each parent graph from memory and label one decisive feature.
Check the domain before drawing any graph involving a root, denominator or logarithm.
Use factorisation to place known polynomial roots accurately.
Do not confuse a quadratic's smooth minimum with a modulus graph's sharp corner.
Next lesson: Graph Transformations.

screens.title: Transform features instead of redrawing from scratch

diagram.caption: A transformed graph keeps the parent graph's structure while moving or rescaling its key features.

screens.body:
The four core forms are [[y=af(x)]], [[y=f(x)+a]], [[y=f(x+a)]], and [[y=f(ax)]].

Track distinctive points, roots, vertices, endpoints, and asymptotes. Their coordinates change by consistent rules.
screens.keyPoint: Outside changes act on y-coordinates. Inside changes act on x-coordinates, with the direction or scale appearing reversed.

screens.title: Adding outside f moves every point vertically

diagram.caption: On y = f(x) + a, every x-coordinate stays fixed while every y-coordinate increases by a.

screens.body:
For [[y=f(x)+a]], the graph moves up by a when [[a>0]] and down by [[\lvert a\rvert]] when [[a<0]].

A point [[(x,y)]] maps to [[(x,y+a)]]. Horizontal asymptotes also move by the same vertical amount.
screens.keyPoint: The transformation changes outputs, so x-coordinates do not move.

screens.question: The point [[(-2,4)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Which point lies on [[y=f(x)-5]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Subtracting 5 changes only the y-coordinate: [[(-2,4)\mapsto(-2,-1)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The change is outside f, so keep x fixed and subtract 5 from the y-coordinate.

screens.title: Adding inside f moves the graph in the opposite direction

diagram.caption: The graph of y = f(x + a) is shifted left by a when a is positive.

screens.body:
For [[y=f(x+a)]], a point [[(x,y)]] maps to [[(x-a,y)]]. Therefore positive a moves the graph left, while negative a moves it right.

The sign looks reversed because the new input must compensate for the change inside the function.
screens.keyPoint: Inside [[+a]] means left by a; inside [[-a]] means right by a.

screens.question: The graph of [[y=f(x)]] has a minimum at [[(3,-2)]]. Where is the corresponding minimum on [[y=f(x+4)]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f(x+4)]] shifts the graph 4 units left, so [[(3,-2)\mapsto(-1,-2)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A positive number inside f moves the graph left. Subtract 4 from the x-coordinate.

screens.title: Multiplying outside f rescales y-values

diagram.caption: Outside multipliers stretch or compress vertically; a negative multiplier also reflects in the x-axis.

screens.body:
For [[y=af(x)]], a point [[(x,y)]] maps to [[(x,ay)]]. If [[\lvert a\rvert>1]], the graph is stretched vertically; if [[0<\lvert a\rvert<1]], it is compressed vertically.

When [[a<0]], all y-values change sign, giving a reflection in the x-axis.
screens.keyPoint: The x-intercepts stay fixed under [[y=af(x)]] provided [[a\ne0]], because multiplying zero still gives zero.

screens.title: Combine a vertical reflection, stretch, and translation
screens.question: The point [[(5,-2)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Where does it move on [[y=-3f(x)+4]]?

diagram.caption: Apply the multiplier to the y-coordinate before applying the vertical translation.

steps.title: Keep the x-coordinate
steps.body: Both changes are outside f, so [[x=5]] is unchanged.

screens.finalAnswer: The image is [[(5,10)]], so the correct option is C.

screens.title: You can now handle every transformation applied outside f.
screens.body:
Vertical translations alter y by addition, while vertical stretches, compressions, and reflections alter y by multiplication.

Next, we apply the reciprocal scaling rule to changes inside f.

screens.title: Multiplying x inside f changes horizontal distances reciprocally

diagram.caption: On y = f(ax), x-coordinates are divided by a, so larger inside multipliers make the graph narrower.

screens.body:
For [[y=f(ax)]], a point [[(x,y)]] maps to [[\left(\frac{x}{a},y\right)]]. Thus [[\lvert a\rvert>1]] gives a horizontal compression with scale factor [[\frac1{\lvert a\rvert}]].

If [[a<0]], the x-coordinate also changes sign, so the graph is reflected in the y-axis.
screens.keyPoint: Inside multipliers act reciprocally: multiply inside by 3, divide x-coordinates by 3.

screens.question: The point [[(-6,7)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Which point lies on [[y=f(-2x)]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Divide the x-coordinate by [[-2]]: [[-6\div(-2)=3]]. The y-coordinate stays 7.
screens.incorrectFeedback: For [[f(ax)]], divide x-coordinates by a. Here [[-6\div(-2)=3]].

screens.title: Transform vertices and asymptotes using the same coordinate rules

steps.title: Start with a known feature
steps.body: Suppose [[y=f(x)]] has a minimum at [[(6,-1)]], a vertical asymptote [[x=9]], and a horizontal asymptote [[y=-1]].

steps.title: Apply the transformation to the minimum
steps.body: Divide x by 3 and add 2 to y: [[(6,-1)\mapsto(2,1)]].

steps.title: Transform the vertical asymptote
steps.body: Its x-coordinate is divided by 3, so [[x=9]] becomes [[x=3]].

steps.title: Transform the horizontal asymptote
steps.body: It moves up by 2, so [[y=-1]] becomes [[y=1]].

screens.question: Which expression reflects [[y=f(x)]] in the y-axis, compresses it horizontally by scale factor [[\frac12]], and then moves it down 3 units?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Horizontal scale factor [[\frac12]] requires an inside multiplier 2; the negative sign reflects in the y-axis, and subtracting 3 moves the graph down.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A horizontal scale factor is the reciprocal of the inside multiplier. Reflection in the y-axis requires a negative inside multiplier.

screens.title: Use one point-mapping rule for a full composition

diagram.caption: For y = A f(B(x − H)) + K, solve the inside equation for the new x-coordinate and transform y outside.

screens.body:
If [[(u,v)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]], then on [[y=A f(B(x-H))+K]] it maps to

[[\left(H+\frac{u}{B},\;Av+K\right)]].

This rule packages horizontal translation, horizontal scaling or reflection, vertical scaling or reflection, and vertical translation.
screens.keyPoint: Do not apply the written operations blindly from left to right. Map x through the inside expression and y through the outside expression.

screens.title: Map a point through four transformations
screens.question: The point [[(8,-3)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Where does it move on [[y=1-2f(4(x-3))]]?

diagram.caption: Set the new inside input equal to the original x-coordinate, then transform the original y-coordinate.

screens.finalAnswer: The image is [[(5,7)]], so the correct option is B.

screens.question: Which sequence transforms [[y=x^{2}]] into [[y=-3(x+2)^{2}+4]]?
options: Right 2, vertical stretch factor 3, reflect in the x-axis, up 4
Left 2, vertical stretch factor 3, reflect in the x-axis, up 4
Left 2, horizontal stretch factor 3, reflect in the y-axis, up 4
Right 2, vertical compression factor [[\frac13]], reflect in the x-axis, down 4
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[x+2]] moves the graph left 2, the factor 3 stretches vertically, the negative reflects in the x-axis, and [[+4]] moves it up.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read inside changes first for horizontal movement, then use the outside multiplier and final vertical translation.

screens.title: Transform decisive features before drawing the curve
screens.body:
Mark transformed roots, vertices, endpoints, and asymptotes first. Then preserve the parent graph's connectivity and overall shape.

A smooth parent graph remains smooth under these transformations, while a sharp point such as the vertex of [[y=\lvert x\rvert]] remains sharp.
screens.keyPoint: A reliable sketch is built from transformed features; the curve is drawn last.

screens.question: The graph of [[y=f(x)]] has a maximum at [[(-4,5)]] and horizontal asymptote [[y=-2]]. Which statement is true for [[y=-\frac12f(-2x)+3]]?
options: It has a maximum at [[(2,\frac12)]] and asymptote [[y=2]].
It has a minimum at [[(2,\frac12)]] and asymptote [[y=4]].
It has a minimum at [[(-8,\frac12)]] and asymptote [[y=4]].
It has a maximum at [[(2,\frac{11}{2})]] and asymptote [[y=-4]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[-4\div(-2)=2]] and [[-\frac12(5)+3=\frac12]]. The negative outside multiplier turns the maximum into a minimum. The asymptote maps to [[-\frac12(-2)+3=4]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide the x-coordinate by the inside multiplier, transform y using the outside rule, and remember that a negative outside multiplier swaps maxima and minima.

screens.title: Graph Transformations: key points
keyPoints: [[f(x)+a]] changes y-coordinates by adding a.
[[f(x+a)]] changes x-coordinates by subtracting a.
[[af(x)]] multiplies y-coordinates by a and reflects in the x-axis when a is negative.
[[f(ax)]] divides x-coordinates by a and reflects in the y-axis when a is negative.
Transform roots, vertices, endpoints, and asymptotes before drawing the final curve.
reviewItems: Outside changes act vertically; inside changes act horizontally.
Horizontal scale factors are reciprocals of inside multipliers.
Use point mappings instead of guessing the transformed shape.
For combined transformations, solve the inside equation for x and transform y outside.
Next lesson: Graph Parameters and Composite Functions.

screens.title: Turn proportional language into an equation
screens.body:
If y is directly proportional to [[x^{n}]], write [[y=kx^{n}]]. If y is inversely proportional to [[x^{n}]], write [[y=\frac{k}{x^{n}}]].

Use one pair of values to determine k, then keep the same k throughout the problem. Repeated percentage growth is also multiplicative: an increase of [[p\%]] for n stages multiplies an initial value V by [[\left(1+\frac{p}{100}\right)^{n}]].
screens.keyPoint: Proportion and growth are multiplicative. A statement such as [[y\propto x^{2}]] does not mean [[y=x^{2}+c]].

screens.title: Use the constant of proportionality

screens.question: The quantity y is inversely proportional to [[\sqrt{x}]]. If x is multiplied by 9, what happens to y?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[y=\frac{k}{\sqrt{x}}]]. Multiplying x by 9 multiplies [[\sqrt{x}]] by 3, so y is divided by 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[y=kx^{-1/2}]]. A factor of 9 in x becomes a factor of [[9^{-1/2}=\frac13]] in y.

screens.title: Length, area, and volume scale by different powers

diagram.caption: A length scale factor of 2 produces an area factor of 4.

screens.body:
For similar figures with length scale factor k, corresponding lengths scale by k, areas by [[k^{2}]], and volumes by [[k^{3}]].

All corresponding angles are equal. Congruent figures are the special case [[k=1]].
screens.keyPoint: Identify whether the quantity is one-, two-, or three-dimensional before applying the scale factor.

screens.title: Scale the volume of a similar solid
screens.question: Two similar solids have corresponding lengths in the ratio [[2:3]]. The smaller has volume 64. Find the larger volume.

diagram.caption: The linear scale factor is one and a half, so the volume factor is its cube.

steps.title: Check the direction
steps.body: The larger solid must have the larger volume, so 216 is sensible.

screens.finalAnswer: The larger volume is 216, so the correct option is D.

screens.question: Two similar shapes have corresponding lengths in the ratio [[1:3]]. What is the ratio of their areas?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Area uses the square of the length factor, so [[1^{2}:3^{2}=1:9]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Square both parts of the length ratio. Cubing would apply to volume, not area.

screens.title: You can convert proportional relationships and scale similar figures.
screens.body:
You have used constants of proportionality and separated length, area, and volume factors.

Next, we bring together angle facts, transformations, measurement, circles, and vectors.

screens.title: Build unfamiliar geometry from standard facts
screens.body:
Angles on a line total 180°, angles around a point total 360°, and vertically opposite angles are equal. Parallel lines create equal corresponding and alternate angles.

An n-sided polygon has interior-angle sum [[180(n-2)]] degrees and exterior-angle sum 360°. A regular polygon therefore has exterior angle [[\frac{360}{n}]] degrees.
screens.keyPoint: Mark equal and supplementary angles directly on the diagram before forming equations.

screens.question: A regular polygon has exterior angle 24°. How many sides does it have?
screens.incorrectFeedback: The exterior angles of any polygon total 360°. Divide 360 by the regular exterior angle.

screens.title: Know what each transformation preserves
screens.body:
Triangles are congruent by SSS, SAS, ASA, or RHS. Rotation, reflection, and translation preserve lengths and angles; enlargement preserves angles but scales lengths.

A negative enlargement scale factor places the image on the opposite side of the centre. A translation is described by a two-dimensional vector.
screens.keyPoint: SSA is not a general congruence criterion. Two sides and a non-included angle may produce different triangles.

screens.question: An enlargement with centre the origin and scale factor -2 maps [[(3,-1)]] to which point?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Multiply both coordinates by -2: [[(3,-1)\mapsto(-6,2)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For an enlargement centred at the origin, multiply the entire position vector by the scale factor.

screens.title: Reduce three-dimensional problems to right triangles
screens.body:
Use [[a^{2}+b^{2}=c^{2}]] in two or three dimensions, one right triangle at a time. A prism has volume equal to cross-sectional area times length.

Also recall [[C=2\pi r]], [[A=\pi r^{2}]], and, in radians, arc length [[r\theta]] and sector area [[\frac12r^{2}\theta]]. Bearings are measured clockwise from north and written with three figures.
screens.keyPoint: Plans and elevations are two-dimensional views. Match visible lengths carefully before applying a formula.

screens.title: Find a cuboid's space diagonal
screens.question: A cuboid has side lengths 3, 4, and 12. Find the distance between opposite vertices.

steps.title: Form a second right triangle
steps.body: The base diagonal 5 and vertical side 12 are perpendicular.

screens.finalAnswer: The space diagonal is 13, so the correct option is B.

screens.title: Use radii, chords, tangents, and subtended angles

diagram.caption: A radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact.

screens.body:
The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference on the same arc. An angle in a semicircle is 90°, angles in the same segment are equal, and opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°.

The angle between a tangent and a chord equals the angle in the alternate segment.
screens.keyPoint: Join a tangent point to the centre: the resulting radius creates a right angle.

screens.question: A chord subtends an angle of 38° at the circumference. What angle does the same chord subtend at the centre?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference, so [[2\times38^{\circ}=76^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For the same arc, double the angle at the circumference to obtain the angle at the centre.

screens.title: Express routes as vector sums
screens.body:
If position vectors of A and B are a and b, then [[\overrightarrow{AB}=\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}]]. The midpoint has position vector [[\frac12(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})]].

Parallel vectors are scalar multiples. This lets you prove collinearity, parallelism, and fixed ratios without measuring a diagram.
screens.keyPoint: A valid vector proof must explain both direction and scalar relationship, not merely quote matching-looking expressions.

screens.question: Points A and B have position vectors a and b. What is the position vector of the midpoint of AB?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The midpoint averages the two position vectors: [[\frac12(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A midpoint is the coordinate-wise average of its endpoints, so average their position vectors.

screens.title: Geometry and Proportion: key points
keyPoints: Translate direct and inverse proportion into equations containing a constant k.
A length factor k produces area factor [[k^{2}]] and volume factor [[k^{3}]].
Combine angle, congruence, similarity, and transformation facts systematically.
Use Pythagoras in stages for three-dimensional distances.
Circle theorems and vector relationships turn diagrams into exact equations.
reviewItems: Decide whether a proportional model uses x, [[x^{2}]], or a reciprocal power.
Separate length, area, and volume scale factors.
Do not use SSA as a general congruence test.
Add helpful radii and chords to circle diagrams.
Return to Learn to choose the next topic.

screens.title: Match the diagram to the type of data
screens.body:
Bar charts and pie charts suit categorical data; vertical line charts suit ungrouped discrete data; line graphs show time series.

Histograms represent grouped numerical data using area, and cumulative-frequency graphs reveal medians, quartiles, and percentiles.
screens.keyPoint: Histogram bars touch because the underlying scale is continuous. Ordinary bar-chart gaps separate categories.

screens.question: Which diagram is most appropriate for displaying the distribution of 500 measured journey times grouped into unequal class intervals?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Journey time is continuous, grouped numerical data. Unequal intervals require frequency density so bar area represents frequency.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The data are continuous and grouped into unequal widths, which is precisely the setting for a frequency-density histogram.

screens.title: Compare centre and spread like for like
screens.body:
For ungrouped data, calculate exact mean, median, mode, and range. For grouped data, use class midpoints to estimate the mean and interpolate or read graphs to estimate quartiles.

Median and interquartile range are a natural pair: median measures typical position and IQR measures the spread of the middle half.
screens.keyPoint: Grouped statistics are estimates because the exact values inside each class are unknown.

screens.title: Estimate a grouped mean
screens.question: Values in intervals [[0\le x<10]], [[10\le x<20]], and [[20\le x<30]] have frequencies 2, 5, and 3. Estimate the mean.
screens.finalAnswer: The estimated mean is 16, so the correct option is C.

screens.question: Set A has median 50 and IQR 8. Set B has median 55 and IQR 14. Which comparison is justified?
options: A has the higher typical value and greater spread.
B has the higher typical value, while A is more consistent.
B is more consistent because its median is larger.
The sets cannot be compared without their means.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. B's larger median indicates a higher typical value. A's smaller IQR indicates less spread in the middle half.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Compare medians with medians and IQRs with IQRs. A smaller IQR means greater consistency.

screens.title: You can choose representations and compare distributions.
screens.body:
You have distinguished data types, estimated grouped means, and paired measures of centre and spread.

Next, we interpret histograms, cumulative frequency, and scatter data.

screens.title: Histogram area represents frequency

diagram.caption: Unequal class widths require frequency density on the vertical axis.

screens.body:
Use [[\text{frequency density}=\frac{\text{frequency}}{\text{class width}}]]. Therefore [[\text{frequency}=\text{class width}\times\text{frequency density}]].

A taller bar need not contain more observations if it is much narrower.
screens.keyPoint: Compare bar areas for frequency, not heights alone.

screens.question: A histogram class has width 5 and frequency density 3. What is its frequency?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Frequency is bar area: [[5\times3=15]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply class width by frequency density.

screens.title: Use positions to estimate quartiles

diagram.caption: Read horizontally from a cumulative position to the curve, then down to the data scale.

screens.body:
For N observations, estimate [[Q_{1}]] at cumulative frequency [[\frac{N}{4}]], the median at [[\frac{N}{2}]], and [[Q_{3}]] at [[\frac{3N}{4}]].

The interquartile range is [[Q_{3}-Q_{1}]].
screens.keyPoint: The vertical axis shows how many observations are at or below a value.

screens.question: A cumulative-frequency graph represents 80 observations. At which cumulative frequencies are [[Q_{1}]], the median, and [[Q_{3}]] read?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The positions are [[\frac{80}{4}=20]], [[\frac{80}{2}=40]], and [[\frac{3(80)}4=60]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use one quarter, one half, and three quarters of the total frequency.

screens.title: Correlation describes association, not cause

diagram.caption: A line of best fit summarises an apparent trend; it does not prove a causal mechanism.

screens.body:
Scatter graphs can show positive, negative, or no correlation. A line of best fit supports interpolation within the observed range.

Extrapolation beyond the data is less reliable, and even strong correlation does not establish that one variable causes the other.
screens.keyPoint: A third variable or selection effect may explain an association.

screens.question: A strong positive correlation is observed between two variables X and Y. Which conclusion is always valid?
options: Increasing X causes Y to increase.
Increasing Y causes X to increase.
The observed values tend to increase together.
Every point lies exactly on a straight line.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Correlation describes an observed tendency. It does not by itself determine causation or require a perfect line.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A scatter graph supports a statement about association, not a causal direction.

screens.title: Statistics: key points
keyPoints: Choose representations according to whether data are categorical, discrete, continuous, or time-based.
Grouped means and graph-read quartiles are estimates because exact values inside each class are unknown.
Histogram frequency is class width multiplied by frequency density.
Compare data sets using like-for-like measures of centre and spread.
Correlation describes association, not causation, and extrapolation beyond the data is risky.
reviewItems: Use bar area—not height alone—to interpret histogram frequency.
Read quartiles at one quarter, one half, and three quarters of the cumulative total.
Pair the median with the interquartile range when comparing distributions.
State clearly when a grouped statistic is only an estimate.
Return to Learn to continue with Probability.

screens.title: Use repeated outcomes to estimate probability
screens.body:
Relative frequency is [[\frac{\text{number of times the event occurs}}{\text{number of trials}}]]. It gives an experimental estimate of probability.

Probabilities lie from 0 to 1. Repeating the same experiment can produce a different short-run outcome, even when the process is fair.
screens.keyPoint: As the number of trials becomes large, relative frequency often settles closer to the theoretical probability, but it need not match it exactly.

screens.question: An event occurs 42 times in 120 trials. What is its relative frequency?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide the number of successful outcomes by the total number of trials.

screens.title: Fair events have equally likely relevant outcomes
screens.body:
A fair process does not favour one equally defined outcome over another. For example, each face of a fair six-sided die has probability [[\frac16]].

Expected frequency is [[\text{number of trials}\times\text{probability}]]. It predicts a long-run average, not a guaranteed result.
screens.keyPoint: An expected count can be non-integer because it describes an average over many repetitions.

screens.question: An event has probability [[0.18]]. How many times is it expected to occur in 500 trials?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[500\times0.18=90]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply the probability by the number of trials.

screens.title: Complete outcome sets have total probability 1
screens.body:
The probabilities of an exhaustive set of mutually exclusive outcomes sum to 1. Therefore a missing probability can be found by subtraction.

List combined outcomes systematically using a table, grid, tree diagram, or Venn diagram. The representation should make every possible outcome visible exactly once.
screens.keyPoint: Mutually exclusive means the outcomes cannot occur together; exhaustive means that one of them must occur.

screens.question: A fair coin and a fair spinner numbered 1, 2 and 3 are used. What is the probability of obtaining heads and an even number?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The six equally likely outcomes are H1, H2, H3, T1, T2 and T3. Only H2 is favourable, so the probability is [[\frac16]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Construct all six equally likely coin-spinner outcomes and count those satisfying both conditions.

screens.title: Multiply along branches and add complete routes

diagram.caption: Each complete route represents a combined outcome.

steps.title: Multiply for successive events
steps.body: [[P(A\text{ and }B)=P(A)P(B\mid A)]]. Multiply the probabilities along one complete route.

steps.title: Add alternative routes
steps.body: Add route probabilities only when the routes are mutually exclusive ways of producing the required event.

steps.title: Identify independence
steps.body: For independent events, the probability on the second stage is unchanged by the first outcome.

steps.title: Update dependent probabilities
steps.body: Without replacement, the contents of the sample space change, so later branch probabilities usually change.

screens.title: Calculate a probability without replacement
screens.question: A bag contains 3 red and 2 blue counters. Two counters are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both are red?

steps.title: First red counter
steps.body: The probability of red first is [[\frac35]].

steps.title: Update the bag
steps.body: After one red is removed, 2 red counters remain among 4 counters.

steps.title: Check dependence
steps.body: The second probability differs from the first because the draw is without replacement.

screens.finalAnswer: The probability is [[\frac3{10}]], so the correct option is B.

screens.title: Restrict the sample space to the given condition
screens.body:
In [[P(A\mid B)]], event B defines the new sample space. The denominator counts only outcomes satisfying B.

Two-way tables, Venn diagrams, frequency trees, and expected frequencies can all make this restriction visible without formal set notation.
screens.keyPoint: Ask: out of the cases satisfying the condition, how many also satisfy the required event?

screens.question: Among 60 students, 30 play sport. Of those 30, 15 also study music. What is the probability that a randomly selected sport player studies music?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Restrict the denominator to the 30 sport players: [[\frac{15}{30}=\frac12]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The condition is that the student plays sport, so use 30—not 60—as the denominator.

screens.title: Probability: key points
keyPoints: Relative frequency estimates probability from repeated experimental outcomes.
Expected frequency is probability multiplied by the number of trials.
Exhaustive mutually exclusive outcomes have probabilities summing to 1.
Multiply along successive branches and add mutually exclusive complete routes.
For conditional probability, restrict the denominator to outcomes satisfying the condition.
reviewItems: Do not treat an expected frequency as a guaranteed count.
List possibility spaces systematically so no outcome is missed or repeated.
Update branch probabilities when sampling without replacement.
Distinguish independent events from mutually exclusive events.
Return to Learn to choose the next topic.

screens.question: The point [[(1,4)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Which point lies on [[y=f(x+2)-3]]?

diagram.caption: Inside changes act horizontally; outside changes act vertically.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f(x+2)]] moves the point left by 2, and subtracting 3 moves it down by 3: [[(1,4)\mapsto(-1,1)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A positive change inside f moves the graph left. The outside subtraction then lowers the y-coordinate.

screens.question: The point [[(4,-3)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Where does it move on [[y=-f(2x)]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f(2x)]] divides x-coordinates by 2, and the outside negative reflects y-values: [[(4,-3)\mapsto(2,3)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide the x-coordinate by the inside multiplier, then negate the y-coordinate.

screens.question: Let [[f(x)=2x+1]] and [[g(x)=x^{2}]]. Find [[g(f(1))]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Evaluate the inner function f first, then apply g to its output.

screens.question: Which statement describes [[y=3(x-2)^{2}-4]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The vertex is [[(2,-4)]], and the positive coefficient means the parabola opens upwards.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read the vertex from completed-square form and use the sign of the squared-term coefficient.

screens.question: The graphs [[y=x^{2}+1]] and [[y=3x-1]] intersect at two points. What is the sum of their x-coordinates?

diagram.caption: Equate the two expressions to find intersection coordinates.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[x^{2}+1=3x-1]] gives [[x^{2}-3x+2=0]], with roots 1 and 2. Their sum is 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Equate the two y-values, rearrange, and factorise the resulting quadratic.

screens.question: The quantity y is directly proportional to [[x^{3}]]. Given [[y=16]] when [[x=2]], find y when [[x=3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find k from the first pair of values before substituting the new x-value.

screens.question: The quantity y is inversely proportional to [[x^{2}]]. If x doubles, by what factor does y change?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[y=kx^{-2}]], so doubling x multiplies y by [[2^{-2}=\frac14]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply the inverse-square power to the scale factor: [[2^{-2}]].

screens.question: Similar solids have corresponding lengths in the ratio [[3:5]]. The smaller volume is 216. Find the larger volume.

diagram.caption: Volume scales with the cube of the length ratio.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The volume factor is [[\left(\frac53\right)^{3}=\frac{125}{27}]], and [[216\times\frac{125}{27}=1000]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Cube the length scale factor from smaller to larger before scaling the volume.

screens.question: Each interior angle of a regular polygon is 156°. How many sides does it have?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The exterior angle is [[180^{\circ}-156^{\circ}=24^{\circ}]], so the number of sides is [[\frac{360}{24}=15]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First convert the interior angle to an exterior angle, then divide 360° by it.

screens.question: Which information is not sufficient in general to prove that two triangles are congruent?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. SSA can produce two different triangles, so it is not a general congruence criterion.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The standard criteria are SSS, SAS, ASA, and RHS. A non-included angle with two sides can be ambiguous.

screens.question: An enlargement with centre the origin and scale factor [[-\frac12]] maps [[(6,-4)]] to which point?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Multiply each coordinate by [[-\frac12]] to obtain [[(-3,2)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For a centre at the origin, multiply the position vector by the scale factor.

screens.question: A cuboid has side lengths 6, 8, and 24. Find its space diagonal.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply Pythagoras in three dimensions by adding the squares of all three perpendicular side lengths.

screens.question: One angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is 112°. What is the opposite angle?

diagram.caption: Opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Opposite angles sum to 180°, so [[180^{\circ}-112^{\circ}=68^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the cyclic-quadrilateral rule that opposite angles are supplementary.

screens.question: Points A and B have position vectors a and b. Which expression equals [[\overrightarrow{AB}]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The route from A to B is destination minus starting position: [[\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Subtract the starting position vector from the destination position vector.

screens.question: Values in classes [[0\le x<10]] and [[10\le x<20]] have frequencies 4 and 6. Estimate the mean.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Using midpoints 5 and 15 gives [[\frac{4(5)+6(15)}{10}=\frac{110}{10}=11]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply each class midpoint by its frequency, add, and divide by the total frequency.

screens.question: A histogram class has width 8 and frequency density 2.5. Find the class frequency.

diagram.caption: In a histogram, bar area equals frequency.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[8\times2.5=20]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply the class width by its frequency density.

screens.question: A cumulative-frequency graph contains 120 observations. At which cumulative frequency should [[Q_{3}]] be read?

diagram.caption: The upper quartile lies three quarters of the way through the ordered data.

screens.incorrectFeedback: The upper quartile is at three quarters of the total frequency.

screens.question: A scatter graph shows strong negative correlation between X and Y. Which statement is justified?

diagram.caption: Correlation describes an association; it does not establish causation.

options: Larger X-values tend to be associated with smaller Y-values.
Increasing X necessarily causes Y to decrease.
Increasing Y necessarily causes X to decrease.
Every point must lie on the line of best fit.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Negative correlation means the variables tend to move in opposite directions. It does not establish a causal mechanism.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Describe the observed association without making a causal claim.

screens.question: A bag contains 4 green and 3 yellow counters. Two are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both are green?

diagram.caption: Without replacement, the probabilities on the second stage change.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac47\times\frac36=\frac{12}{42}=\frac27]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: After one green counter is removed, 3 green counters remain among 6 counters.

screens.question: Of 50 students, 20 study physics. Of those 20, 8 also study economics. Find [[P(\text{economics}\mid\text{physics})]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Restrict the sample space to the 20 physics students: [[\frac8{20}=\frac25]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The condition is physics, so the denominator must be the number of physics students, not all 50 students.

screens.title: Geometry and Data: exam review
keyPoints: Track points through graph transformations and evaluate composite functions from the inside out.
Translate proportion into equations and apply squared or cubed scale factors where appropriate.
Combine angle, congruence, similarity, circle, coordinate, and vector facts carefully.
Interpret grouped data through midpoints, density, cumulative positions, and honest statements about correlation.
For probability, update dependent branches and restrict conditional denominators.
reviewItems: Redo transformation questions using point mappings.
Label the dimension of every similarity factor.
Draw or annotate geometry before calculating.
Use histogram area rather than height alone.
Repeat dependent and conditional probability questions until the denominator is automatic.

screens.title: A mathematical statement has a definite truth value
screens.body:
A statement is a claim that is either true or false. “[[7]] is prime” is true; “[[9]] is prime” is false. A question or an instruction is not a statement.

Compound statements use and, or, and not. In mathematics, “or” is inclusive: “A or B” allows A, B, or both to be true.
screens.keyPoint: “A and B” is true only when both parts are true. “A or B” is false only when both parts are false.

screens.title: Read compound claims one word at a time

steps.title: And requires both
steps.body: The claim “[[n]] is positive and even” requires both properties. A positive odd number does not satisfy it.

steps.title: Or includes the overlap
steps.body: The claim “[[n]] is divisible by [[2]] or [[3]]” includes multiples of [[6]], because both conditions may hold.

steps.title: Not reverses the truth value
steps.body: The negation of “[[x\gt4]]” is “[[x\le4]]”, not merely “[[x\lt4]]”. Equality must be included.

screens.question: Which integer satisfies “divisible by [[2]] or divisible by [[3]]” because both parts are true?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[6]] is divisible by both [[2]] and [[3]], and inclusive “or” allows this overlap.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Look for a number divisible by both [[2]] and [[3]], not exactly one of them.

screens.title: An if-then statement points in one direction
screens.body:
“If A, then B” promises that whenever A is true, B must also be true. A is the condition and B is the conclusion.

The claim says nothing about cases where A is false. It also does not automatically allow the direction to be reversed.
screens.keyPoint: To disprove “if A, then B”, find a case where A is true but B is false.

diagram.caption: The contrapositive reverses and negates both parts, preserving the original meaning.

screens.title: Small words decide the direction

steps.title: “A if B”
steps.body: This means: if B is true, then A is true. B is sufficient for A.

steps.title: “A only if B”
steps.body: This means: if A is true, then B must be true. B is necessary for A.

steps.title: “A if and only if B”
steps.body: Both directions must hold: if A then B, and if B then A.
